Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (١)
Those who are [themselves] faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah—He has rendered their works fruitless. (1)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَا نُزِّلَ عَلَىٰ مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۙ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بَالَهُمْ (٢)
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—He shall absolve them of their misdeeds and set right their affairs. (2)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ (٣)
That is because the faithless follow falsehood, and the faithful follow the truth from their Lord. That is how Allah draws comparisons for mankind. (3)

Content of Surah Muhammad (ص)

This Surah is named "Muhammad" (ص) because the name of the Prophet of Islam is mentioned in its second verse. Its other name is Surah "Qital" (The Surah of Fighting), and indeed, the issue of Jihad and war against the enemies of Islam is the most significant theme overshadowing this Surah. Meanwhile, another substantial portion of its verses deals with comparing the state of the believers and the disbelievers, their characteristics and attributes, as well as their destiny in the Hereafter.

In general, the content of the Surah can be summarized into several sections:

  1. The issue of faith (Iman) and disbelief (Kufr), and a comparison between the state of the believers and the disbelievers in this world and the next.

  2. Eloquent and explicit discussions regarding the issue of Jihad and battling enemies, and instructions concerning prisoners of war.

  3. Another considerable part describes the state of the hypocrites (Munafiqun), who were engaged in many subversive activities in Medina at the time these verses were revealed.

  4. Another section speaks of "traveling through the land" and examining the fate of past nations as a lesson to be learned.

  5. In a portion of the verses, the issue of Divine trial is raised in relation to the matter of war.

  6. In another part, the issue of "spending in the way of Allah" (Infaq)—which is also a form of Jihad—is discussed, along with its opposite, "miserliness" (Bukhl).

  7. In some verses, in this same context, the issue of peace with the disbelievers (a peace that leads to defeat and humiliation) is raised and forbidden.

Altogether, considering that this Surah was revealed in Medina during the intense conflict between Muslims and the enemies of Islam—and according to a group of commentators, it was in the heat of the Battle of Uhud or shortly thereafter—the primary subject is war. Other matters revolve around this axis: a decisive war that distinguishes the ranks of the believers from the disbelievers and hypocrites; a war that strengthened the foundations of Islam and put in their place the enemies who intended to destroy Islam and the Muslims.

The Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam (ص), it is stated: "Whoever recites Surah Muhammad (ص), it is a right upon Allah to quench his thirst from the rivers of Paradise." [^1] In the book Thawab al-A'mal, it is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: "Whoever recites Surah 'Al-ladhina Kafaru' (Surah Muhammad ص), doubt and hesitation shall never enter his heart regarding his religion, Allah shall never afflict him with poverty in religion, nor shall he ever fear a ruler, and he will remain protected from polytheism and disbelief until he dies. When he dies, Allah appoints a thousand angels in his grave to pray therein, and the reward of their prayers shall be for him, and they will accompany him until they station him at the station of security before Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, and he will be under the protection of Allah and the protection of Muhammad (ص)." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, beginning of Surah Muhammad (ص). [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 25.

It is clear that those who implement the content of these verses in their souls and do not allow doubt or wavering in the struggle against stubborn, ruthless, and illogical enemies, will find both the foundations of their religion and faith strengthened, and fear, humiliation, and poverty will be removed from them, and in the Resurrection, they will enjoy the proximity of Divine mercy.

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that the Imam (عليه السلام) said: "Whoever wishes to know our state and the state of our enemies, let him recite Surah Muhammad, for he will find one verse concerning us and one verse concerning them." [^1] This Hadith has also been narrated by Sunni commentators such as Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani [^2] and Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur [^3].

This reflects the reality that the most perfect examples of faith were the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet (ص), and the most prominent examples of disbelief and hypocrisy were the Umayyads. It is true that there is no explicit mention of the Ahl al-Bayt or the Umayyads by name in this Surah, but since it discusses the two groups of believers and hypocrites and their characteristics, it refers first and foremost to those clear instances, while simultaneously not preventing the Surah from encompassing all other believers and hypocrites.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, beginning of the Surah. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 33. [^3]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 46.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ (1) وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ آمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى‌ مُحَمَّدٍ وَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَ أَصْلَحَ بالَهُمْ (2) ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْباطِلَ وَ أَنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْحَقَّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثالَهُمْ (3)

Commentary

Believers Follow the Truth and Disbelievers Follow Falsehood

These three verses are, in fact, an introduction to an important military command given in the fourth verse. In the first verse, He describes the state of the disbelievers; in the second verse, the state of the believers; and in the third verse, He compares the two, so that by clarifying these lines, readiness for an ideological struggle against ruthless and oppressive enemies is achieved.

First, He says: "Those who disbelieve and bar [men] from the way of Allah, He brings their deeds to naught and makes them go astray" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ). This refers to the leaders of disbelief and the polytheists of Mecca who were the instigators of anti-Islamic wars; not only were they disbelievers themselves, but they also barred others from the way of Allah through various tricks and schemes.

Although some commentators, like Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, have interpreted "Sadda" here to mean "turning away" from faith—in contrast to the next verse which speaks of faith—given the usage of this word in the Holy Quran, its original meaning, which is "preventing and barring," should be maintained.

The meaning of "He brings their deeds to naught" (أضل اعمالهم) is that He destroys and nullifies them, because "making something go astray" is a metaphor for leaving something without a guardian, the consequence of which is its perishing.

In any case, some commentators consider this sentence to refer to those who slaughtered camels and distributed them to the people on the day of the Battle of Badr; Abu Jahl slaughtered ten camels, Safwan ten, and Sahl ibn Amr ten for the army. [^1] However, because these deeds were in the path of polytheism and satanic programs, they were all nullified.

But it appears that it is not limited to this meaning; rather, all the deeds they performed ostensibly to help the needy, or out of hospitality or otherwise, are all nullified due to their lack of faith. Furthermore, the actions they took to erase Islam and crush the Muslims—Allah made all of those go astray and perish as well, preventing them from reaching their destination and goal.

The next verse is a description of the state of the believers, who stand in direct contrast to the disbelievers whose attributes were mentioned in the previous verse. He says: "And those who believe and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—He will remit from them their sins and amend their state" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ آمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى‌ مُحَمَّدٍ وَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَ أَصْلَحَ بالَهُمْ). [^2] Mentioning "faith in what has been sent down to the Prophet of Islam (ص)" after mentioning faith in absolute terms is an emphasis on the programs of this great Prophet, and is of the nature of mentioning the specific after the general. It reflects the reality that without faith in what was revealed to the Prophet (ص), faith in Allah is never complete.

There is also the possibility that the first phrase refers to faith in Allah and has a doctrinal aspect, while this phrase refers to faith in the content of Islam and its teachings and has a practical aspect. In other words, faith in Allah alone is not enough; rather, they must also have faith in "what was sent down to him"—faith in the Quran, faith in Jihad, faith in prayer and fasting, and faith in the moral values revealed to him. A faith that is the source of movement and an emphasis on righteous action.

It is noteworthy that after mentioning this phrase, He says: "and it is the truth from their Lord" (وَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ). That is, their faith is not without basis or reason; they believed because they recognized the truth within it. And the expression "from their Lord" (من ربهم) is an emphasis on the reality that truth is always from the Lord; it originates from Him and returns to Him.

It is also noteworthy that in contrast to the two punishments mentioned for the disbelievers who bar from the way of Allah, He mentions two rewards for the righteous believers: the first is the covering of lapses and the forgiveness of errors from which no non-infallible human is free, and the other is "amending their state" (islah al-bal).

"Bal" has various meanings: state, work, heart; and according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it means "matters of great importance." Therefore, "amending the state" (islah al-bal) means organizing all affairs of life and destiny-making matters, which naturally includes victory in this world and salvation in the Hereafter—the opposite of the fate of the disbelievers whose efforts and endeavors lead nowhere and yield nothing but defeat, as per "He brings their deeds to naught."

It can be said that the forgiveness of sins is the result of their faith, and the amendment of their state is the result of their righteous deeds. Believers possess both peace of mind and success in practical programs, as "amending the state" has a wide scope and includes all of these. And what blessing is greater than for a person to have a calm soul, a reassured heart, and useful, constructive programs?

In the final verse, He states the core reason for this victory and that defeat in a concise and eloquent comparison, saying: "That is because those who disbelieve follow falsehood, and those who believe follow the truth from their Lord" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْباطِلَ وَ أَنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْحَقَّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ). The heart of the matter is that the two lines of "faith" and "disbelief" branch out from the two lines of "truth" and "falsehood." "Truth" (Haqq) means objective realities, the highest of which is the Pure Essence of the Lord, followed by the truths related to human life, the laws governing his relationship with God, and their relationships with one another.

"Falsehood" (Batil) means delusions, fantasies, deceptions, superstitious legends, futile and aimless actions, and any deviation from the laws governing the universe. Yes, the believers follow the truth in the sense mentioned, and the disbelievers follow falsehood, and this is the reason for the victory of the former and the defeat of the latter.

The Holy Quran says: "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that which is between them in vain (falsehood)" (Surah Sad: 27). Some have interpreted "falsehood" to mean "Satan," and some to mean "futility," but as we said, "falsehood" has a broad meaning that includes all of these and more.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "Thus does Allah set forth for mankind their descriptions" (كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثالَهُمْ). That is, just as He has explained the lines of life for the believers and disbelievers, their beliefs, practical programs, and the results of their work in these verses, He defines the destiny of their lives and the outcome of their affairs.

"Raghib" in Al-Mufradat says: "Mathal" (parable/example) means a statement said about something similar to a statement said about a comparable matter, so that one clarifies the other. From his other remarks, it is also understood that this word is sometimes used to mean "similarity" and sometimes to mean "description." Apparently, in the verse under discussion, the second meaning is intended; that is, Allah thus describes the state of the people, just as it appears in verse 15 of Surah Muhammad: "The description (mathal) of the Paradise promised to the righteous is such..." In any case, it is clearly understood from this verse that the closer we are to the truth, the closer we are to faith, and to whatever extent our belief and action incline toward falsehood, the further we are from the reality of faith and the closer to disbelief! For the line of faith and disbelief is the same as the line of truth and falsehood.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَضَرْبَ ٱلرِّقَابِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا۟ ٱلْوَثَاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّۢا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَآءً حَتَّىٰ تَضَعَ ٱلْحَرْبُ أَوْزَارَهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَٱنتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَا۟ بَعْضَكُم بِبَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَن يُضِلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٤)
When you meet the faithless in battle, strike their necks. When you have thoroughly decimated them, bind the captives firmly. Thereafter either oblige them [by setting them free] or take ransom, until the war lays down its burdens. That [is Allah’s ordinance]. Had Allah wished He could have taken vengeance on them, but that He may test some of you by means of others. As for those who were slain in the way of Allah, He will not let their works go fruitless. (4)
سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بَالَهُمْ (٥)
He will guide them and set right their affairs, (5)
وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ (٦)
and admit them into paradise, with which He has acquainted them. (6)

Commentary

Decisiveness is Necessary on the Battlefield

As previously mentioned, the preceding verses served as a preamble to prepare the Muslims for the declaration of an important military command presented in the verses under discussion. It states: "So, when you meet the disbelievers [in battle], strike [their] necks with all your might!" (فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ) [^1]. It is evident that "striking the necks" is an allusion to killing; therefore, it is not necessary for warriors to exert their efforts specifically on this particular act. The objective is to neutralize the enemy, but since beheading is the clearest manifestation of killing, it has been emphasized.

In any case, this ruling pertains to the field of battle, because the word laqitum (from the root liqa') in such contexts means "war." Multiple clues within the verse itself—such as the issue of "taking captives," the word harb (war), and "martyrdom in the way of Allah" mentioned at the end of the verse—testify to this meaning.

In short, liqa' is sometimes used to mean any kind of meeting, and sometimes to mean confronting one another on the battlefield; in the Holy Quran, it is used in both senses, and the verse under discussion refers to the second meaning.

[^1]: Darb is an infinitive (masdar) acting as an absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq) for an omitted verb, and the implied structure is: idribu darba al-riqab (strike a striking of the necks), just as it is explicitly stated in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 12: "فَاضْرِبُوا فَوْقَ الْأَعْناقِ" (So strike them over the necks).

From this, it becomes clear that individuals who, for the purpose of anti-Islamic propaganda, have interpreted the verse to mean that Islam says: "Strike the neck of every disbeliever you meet!" are doing nothing but exercising bias and ill intent. Otherwise, the verse itself is explicit regarding the issue of confrontation on the battlefield.

It is obvious that when a person faces a bloodthirsty enemy on the battlefield, if he does not deliver hard attacks and crushing blows with the utmost decisiveness, he himself will be destroyed. Thus, this instruction is a completely logical one.

Then it adds: These crushing attacks must continue until you have sufficiently crushed the enemy and brought them to their knees; at this point, proceed to take captives and bind them firmly (حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ).

Athkhantumuhum is derived from the root thikhan (on the weight of shikan), meaning thickness and solidity. For this reason, it is applied to clear victory, triumph, and complete dominance over the enemy.

Although most commentators have taken this phrase to mean a high frequency and intensity of killing the enemy, as we have said, this meaning is not in its linguistic root. However, since the danger is sometimes not removed except through intense and widespread killing of the enemy, one of the instances of this phrase in such conditions could be the issue of killing, though it is not its primary concept [^1]. In any case, the above verse expresses a calculated military instruction: that before the definitive breaking of the enemy's resistance, one should not proceed to take captives, because attending to this matter can sometimes cause the Muslims' position in the war to falter, and dealing with captives and evacuating them behind the front lines distracts them from their primary duty.

The expression fashuddu al-wathaq (considering that wathaq means a rope or anything

[^1]: In Lisan al-Arab, it is narrated from Ibn al-A'rabi that "athkhana is when he triumphs and conquers."

used for binding) refers to being meticulous in binding the captives, lest a captive takes advantage of an opportunity to free himself and deliver a fatal blow.

In the next sentence, it states the ruling for prisoners of war that must be implemented after the conclusion of the war, saying: either show them favor and release them without compensation, or take a ransom and compensation from them and release them (فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَ إِمَّا فِداءً).

Thus, a prisoner of war cannot be killed after the end of the war; rather, the leader of the Muslims, according to the interests he considers, releases them sometimes without compensation and sometimes with compensation. This compensation is, in reality, a type of war indemnity that the enemy must pay.

Of course, there is a third ruling in this regard in Islam, which is to take the captives as slaves, but that is not a mandatory command; rather, it is only if the leader of the Muslims deems it necessary under specific conditions and circumstances. Perhaps for this reason, it is not explicitly mentioned in the text of the Quran and is only reflected in Islamic narrations.

Our famous jurist, Fadil Miqdad, says in Kanz al-Irfan: "What has been narrated from the school of the Ahl al-Bayt is that if the captive is taken after the end of the war, the Imam of the Muslims has a choice between three actions: unconditional release, taking a ransom and releasing, or enslaving them; in any case, killing them is not permissible."

In another part of his discourse, he says: "The issue of slavery is derived from narrations, not from the text of the verse" [^1]. This matter has also appeared in other books of jurisprudence [^2].

[^1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 365. [^2]: Sharayi', Book of Jihad; Sharh al-Lum'ah, Rulings of Spoils.

In the discussion on "Slavery" which will follow these verses, we will refer to this discussion again.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, it adds: "This situation must continue, and the enemies must be crushed, and a group taken into captivity until the war lays down its heavy burdens" (حَتَّى تَضَعَ الْحَرْبُ أَوْزارَها) [^1]. Only stop when you have broken the enemy's ability to resist and the fire of war is extinguished.

Awzar is the plural of wizr, meaning "heavy burden," and it is sometimes applied to "sins" as well, because they are also a heavy burden on the shoulders of their bearer.

Interestingly, these heavy burdens in the verse are attributed to "war," saying: "war lays down its burdens." These heavy burdens are an allusion to the various "weapons" and "hardships" that the warriors carry on their shoulders and face, and until the war ends, this burden remains on their shoulders.

But when does the war between Islam and disbelief end? This is a question to which commentators have given different answers.

Some, like Ibn Abbas, have said: It is until the time when idolatry no longer remains on the face of the earth and the creed of polytheism is dismantled.

Others have said: The war between Islam and disbelief continues until the Muslims are victorious over the "Dajjal," based on a hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "And Jihad continues from the time Allah sent me until the last of my Ummah fights the Dajjal" (و الجهاد ماض مذ بعثنى اللَّه الى ان يقاتل آخر امتى الدجال) [^1]. The discussion regarding the "Dajjal" is an extensive one, but it is known that the "Dajjal" is a deceptive man or deceptive men who, in the end times, will be active in diverting people from the principle of monotheism, truth, and justice, and Imam Mahdi عليه السلام will crush them with His great power.

Thus, as long as the Dajjals live on the face of the earth, the battle between truth and falsehood continues! In reality, Islam has two types of battle with disbelief: one is periodic battles, such as the expeditions the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had with enemies, where after the end of each war, swords were sheathed; and the other is the continuous battle it has with "polytheism, disbelief, oppression, and corruption," and this is a continuous matter until the time of the expansion of the global government of justice by Imam Mahdi عليه السلام.

Then it adds: "Your program is thus" (ذلِكَ) [^2] "and had Allah willed, He could have taken vengeance upon them [by other means]" (وَ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللَّهُ لَانْتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ).

Through heavenly lightning, earthquakes, storms, and other calamities; but in that case, the field of trial would be closed. "But [He willed it thus] so that He may test some of you by means of others" (وَ لكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ).

This is, in fact, the philosophy of war and the core point of the conflict between truth and falsehood. In these battles, the ranks of the true believers and those who are people of action are separated from the people of mere words.

Talents flourish, the power of endurance and steadfastness is revitalized, and the primary

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 98. [^2]: Dhalika is the predicate (khabar) of an omitted subject (mubtada'), and the implied structure is al-amru dhalika (the matter is thus).

goal of worldly life—which is the testing and cultivation of the power of faith and other human values—is fulfilled.

If the believers were to sit aside and be occupied with repetitive daily life, and whenever a group of polytheists and oppressors rose up, Allah were to crush them with unseen power and through miracles, a worthless, stagnant, lethargic, weak, and incapable society would emerge that possessed nothing more than the name of faith and Islam.

In summary, Allah does not need our fighting to establish His religion; it is we who are cultivated in the field of struggle against the enemy and are in need of this sacred battle.

This same meaning is recounted in other verses of the Quran in different forms. In Surah Al-Imran, verse 142, we read: "أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَ لَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ يَعْلَمَ الصَّابِرِينَ": "Or did you think that you would enter Paradise while Allah has not yet made evident those of you who fight [in His cause] and made evident those who are steadfast?"

And in the verse before it: "وَ لِيُمَحِّصَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ يَمْحَقَ الْكافِرِينَ": "The goal is that Allah may purify those who believe (in the shadow of these battles) and destroy the disbelievers."

In the final sentence of the verse under discussion, it speaks of the martyrs who lose their sweet lives in these battles and hold a great right over the Islamic society, saying: "As for those who are killed in the way of Allah, He will never let their deeds be lost" (وَ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَلَنْ يُضِلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

Their efforts, sufferings, and sacrifices do not vanish; all are preserved in the presence of Allah. In this world too, the effects of their sacrifices remain; every cry of "La ilaha illa Allah" that reaches the ear is the product of their efforts, and every Muslim who bows his head in prostration before Allah does so by the blessing of their sacrifice. The chains of captivity were broken by their efforts, and the honor and dignity of the Muslims are indebted to them.

This is one of the divine favors regarding the martyrs.

And it adds three other favors in the following verses. First, it says: "He will guide them" (سَيَهْدِيهِمْ). Guidance to the high stations, the great triumph, and the pleasure of Allah (Ridwan Allah).

Another is that "He will amend their state" (وَ يُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ). He grants them peace of soul, tranquility of heart, and spiritual and religious vitality, and makes them harmonious with the purity and spirituality of the divine angels who are their companions. And in the proximity of His mercy, He invites them to His banquet.

And the final favor is that: "He will admit them into the eternal Paradise which He has made known to them" (وَ يُدْخِلُهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَها لَهُمْ). Some commentators have said that He has not only described the general characteristics of the high Paradise and the Garden of Pleasure to them, but He also specifies the descriptions and signs of their heavenly palaces such that when they enter Paradise, they go straight toward their own palaces! [^1] Some have also interpreted arrafaha from the root arf (on the weight of fikr) meaning "fragrance and pleasant scent," meaning Allah admits them into a Paradise that He has perfumed entirely for His guests.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 98.

But the first interpretation seems more appropriate. Some have also said that if we combine these verses with the verse "وَ لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْواتاً" (Al-Imran: 169), it becomes clear that the meaning of "amending the state" is that eternal life under whose shadow the martyrs become ready for presence before the Lord, with the removal of veils and curtains [^1].

Key Points

1- The High Station of the Martyrs

In the history of nations, days arise when dangers are not averted and great, sacred goals are not preserved without sacrifice, devotion, and the giving of many victims. It is here that a group of believers and self-sacrificing individuals must enter the field and guard the religion of truth by offering their blood. In the logic of Islam, such individuals are called "Shahid" (Martyr).

The application of "Shahid" (from the root shuhud) to them is either because of their presence (hudur) on the battlefield against the enemies of truth, or because at the moment of martyrdom they witness the angels of mercy, or because of witnessing the great blessings prepared for them, or their presence before Allah, as stated in the noble verse: "وَ لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْواتاً بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ" (Al-Imran: 169).

In Islam, few people reach the level of a "Shahid"—those martyrs who consciously and with sincerity of intention went toward the battlefield of truth and falsehood and offered the last drops of their pure blood.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 18, p. 344.

Regarding the station of the martyrs, extraordinary narrations are found in Islamic sources that speak of the tremendous value of the martyrs' work. In a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Above every good deed, there is a better good deed, until a man is killed as a martyr in the way of Allah, above which nothing better can be imagined" (ان فوق كل بر برا حتى يقتل الرجل شهيدا فى سبيل اللَّه) [^1]. In another hadith narrated from Him: "The mujahideen in the way of Allah are the leaders of the people of Paradise!" (المجاهدون فى اللَّه قواد اهل الجنة) [^2]. In another hadith from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "There is no drop more beloved to Allah than a drop of blood shed in His way, or a drop of tears from the eye in the darkness of the night out of fear of Allah; and there is no step more beloved to Allah than a step taken toward a relative (to maintain ties), or a step by which a rank in the way of Allah is completed!" (ما من قطرة احب الى اللَّه من قطرة دم فى سبيل اللَّه، او قطرة من دموع عين فى سواد الليل من خشية اللَّه، و ما من قدم احب الى اللَّه من خطوة الى ذى رحم، او خطوة يتم بها زحفا فى سبيل اللَّه) [^3]. If we turn the pages of Islamic history, we see that a significant part of the honors was created by the martyrs, and a vast portion of the service was rendered by them.

Not only yesterday, but today as well, it is the destiny-making culture of "martyrdom" that sends shivers down the spines of the enemies and makes them despair of penetrating the fortresses of Islam. How blessed is the culture of martyrdom for Muslims, and how terrifying it is for the enemies of Islam.

[^1] and [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 100, p. 15. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 100, p. 14.

But undoubtedly, "martyrdom" is not a "goal"; the goal is victory over the enemy and guarding the religion of truth. However, these guardians must be so prepared that if the sacrifice of blood is necessary on this path, they do not withhold it. This is the meaning of a martyr-nurturing nation, not that they seek martyrdom as a goal in itself.

For this reason, at the end of a detailed hadith narrated by the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the station of the martyrs, we read: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله swore: "By the One in Whose hand is my soul, if the Prophets were on their path, they would dismount for them because of the splendor they see from them; and one man among them shall intercede for seventy thousand of his family and neighbors" (و الذى نفسى بيده لو كان الانبياء فى طريقهم لترجلوا لهم لما يرون من بهائهم و يشفع الرجل منهم سبعين الفا من اهل بيته و جيرته) [^1]. It is also noteworthy that martyrdom in Islamic culture has two different meanings: a "specific" meaning and a broad, "general" meaning.

The specific meaning of martyrdom is being killed in the heat of battle in the way of Allah, which has specific rulings in Islamic jurisprudence, including the martyr's lack of need for ritual bathing (ghusl) and shrouding (kafan); rather, he is buried in those same blood-stained clothes!

But the broad meaning of martyrdom is that a person is killed or dies while performing a divine duty; anyone who passes away in any manner while performing such a duty is a "Shahid." Therefore, it is mentioned in Islamic narrations that several groups die as martyrs:

1- It is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam: "When death comes to a seeker of knowledge while he is in

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 100, p. 14.

this state, he dies as a martyr" (اذا جاء الموت طالب العلم و هو على هذا الحال مات شهيدا) [^1]. 2- The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "Whoever among you dies in his bed while possessing knowledge of the right of his Lord and the right of His Messenger and his Ahl al-Bayt, dies as a martyr" (من مات منكم على فراشه و هو على معرفة حق ربه و حق رسوله و اهل بيته مات شهيدا) [^2]. 3- In another hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever is killed defending his property is a martyr" (من قتل دون ماله فهو شهيد) [^3]. And likewise, others who are killed or die on the path of truth. From here, the greatness of this Islamic culture and its expansion becomes clear.

We conclude this discussion with a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: He narrates from His fathers from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "The first to enter Paradise is the martyr" (اول من يدخل الجنة الشهيد) [^4].

2- The Goals of War in Islam

War in Islam is never regarded as a "value" in itself; rather, from the perspective that it is a source of destruction and the loss of lives, forces, and resources, it is considered an "anti-value." Therefore, in some verses of the Quran, it is placed in the same category as divine punishments. In Surah

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, root sh-h-d. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 190 (end of the sermon). [^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, root sh-h-d. [^4]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 71, p. 272.

Al-An'am, verse 65, we read: "قُلْ هُوَ الْقادِرُ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذاباً مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعاً وَ يُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُمْ بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ": "Say, 'He is the [one] Able to send upon you punishment from above you or from beneath your feet or to confound you [into] sects and make some of you taste the violence of others.'"

Here, war is counted as something in the same category as "lightning," "earthquakes," and earthly and heavenly calamities. For this reason, in Islam, war is avoided as much as possible.

But where the existence of a nation is endangered, or its high sacred goals are at risk of collapse, here war becomes a value and takes on the title of "Jihad in the way of Allah."

For this reason, there are types of Jihad in Islam: "initial liberatory Jihad," "defensive Jihad," and "Jihad to extinguish the fire of sedition, polytheism, and idolatry," the details of which we have explained elsewhere [^1]. Therefore, Islamic Jihad, contrary to what malicious enemies propagate, is never for the purpose of imposing belief; fundamentally, imposed belief has no value in Islam. Rather, Jihad pertains to cases where the enemy imposes war on the Islamic nation, or takes away its God-given freedoms, or wants to trample its rights, or where an oppressor throttles an oppressed person, such that it is incumbent upon Muslims to rush to the aid of the oppressed, even if it leads to conflict with the oppressive people.

In the previous verses, this meaning was also reflected in a subtle and short phrase where it says those who disbelieve follow falsehood and the believers

[^1]: Vol. 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 14 onwards (under verse 193 of Al-Baqarah).

follow the truth. Thus, the war is a war of truth and falsehood, not a means for conquest, expansionism, plundering the wealth of others, bullying, or tyranny.

Also for this reason, in the narration we cited in the commentary of the verses, we read that the fire of war is not extinguished in human society until the Dajjals are crushed and the environment of the earth is cleansed of the filth of their existence.

It is noteworthy that in Islam, the issue of peaceful coexistence with the followers of other heavenly religions is emphasized, and detailed discussions have appeared in the verses, narrations, and Islamic jurisprudence in this regard under the title of "Rulings of the People of the Dhimmah."

If Islam were a proponent of imposing belief and resorting to force and the sword for the advancement of its goals, what would be the meaning of the law of the People of the Dhimmah and peaceful coexistence?

3- Rulings of Prisoners of War

We said that Muslims should never think of taking captives before the complete defeat of the enemy on the battlefield—an act that, in any case, carries heavy risks.

However, the tone of the verses under discussion testifies that after victory over the enemy, one should proceed to take them captive instead of killing them. Thus, it says: "When you meet the enemy, deliver your heavy blows upon them."

Then it adds: When you have sufficiently crushed their power, proceed to take and bind them: "فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ".

In this way, after dominance, they should be taken captive instead of being killed—an act that is unavoidable, because if the enemy is released, they might reorganize their forces and launch a fresh attack.

But after captivity, the scene changes, and the captive, despite all the crimes he has committed, becomes a divine trust in the hands of the Muslims, and many rights must be observed regarding him.

The Holy Quran honors and celebrates those who sacrificed and gave their food to a captive, saying: "وَ يُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى‌ حُبِّهِ مِسْكِيناً وَ يَتِيماً وَ أَسِيراً": "And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive" (this verse, according to the famous narration, was revealed regarding Imam Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn عليهم السلام who were fasting and gave their iftar food to the needy, the orphan, and the captive).

Even regarding captives who are exceptionally executed because of their dangerous nature or due to the commission of specific crimes, it is commanded to be kind to them before the execution of the sentence. As we read in a hadith from Ali عليه السلام: "Feeding the captive and being kind to him is an obligatory right, even if you are to kill him the next day" (اطعام الاسير و الاحسان اليه حق واجب و ان قتلته من الغد) [^1]. And the hadiths in this regard are many [^2]. Even in a hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is mentioned that He said: "When you have taken a captive and he becomes unable to walk, and you do not have a mount with you, then release him and do not kill him,

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 11, p. 69. [^2]: Refer to Furu' al-Kafi, Vol. 5, p. 35, Chapter on Kindness to the Captive and Feeding Him.

for you do not know what the Imam's ruling will be regarding him" (اذا اخذت اسيرا فعجز عن المشى و ليس معك محمل فارسله، و لا تقتله، فانك لا تدرى ما حكم الامام فيه) [^1]. It is even recorded in the history of the leaders of Islam that they would give the captives the same food they themselves consumed.

As for the ruling of the captive, as we said in the commentary of the verses, after the conclusion of the war it is one of three things: unconditional release, release conditional on paying a ransom (indemnity), or enslaving him. Of course, the choice of one of these three matters is subject to the opinion of the Imam and leader of the Muslims. He, considering the conditions of the captives and the interests of Islam and the Muslims internally and externally, chooses and orders the implementation of what is most appropriate.

Therefore, neither taking indemnity is mandatory nor is enslaving; rather, these are subject to the interests that the Imam of the Muslims foresees. Whenever it is not in the best interest, he waives it and releases the captives without indemnity or slavery.

Regarding the philosophy of taking ransom, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 7, page 250 onwards (under verse 70 of Surah Al-Anfal).

4- Slavery in Islam

Although the issue of istiqraq (taking and keeping slaves) has not appeared in the Holy Quran as a mandatory command regarding prisoners of war, it cannot be denied

[^1]: Same source.

that rulings for slaves are mentioned in the Quran, which proves the existence of slavery even during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the early days of Islam—such as the rulings regarding marriage with slaves, or rulings of mahramiyat (unmarriageable kin), or the issue of mukatabah (a contract for the freedom of slaves), which have appeared in numerous verses of the Quran in Surahs An-Nisa, An-Nahl, Al-Mu'minun, An-Nur, Ar-Rum, and Al-Ahzab.

It is here that some criticize Islam, asking why this divine religion, with all its content and high human values, did not completely abolish the issue of slavery and declare the freedom of all slaves through a definitive and general decree?!

It is true that Islam gave many recommendations regarding slaves, but what is important is their unconditional freedom. Why should one human be the property of another and lose freedom, which is the greatest divine gift?!

Answer: In a short sentence, it must be said that Islam has a precise and timed program for the freedom of slaves, such that eventually all of them are gradually freed without this freedom creating an undesirable reaction in society.

But before we proceed to explain this precise Islamic plan, we deem it necessary to mention a few points as a preamble.

1- Islam was never the inventor of slavery

Islam was never the inventor of slavery; rather, it emerged at a time when the issue of slavery had encompassed the entire world and was woven into the fabric of human societies. Even after Islam, the issue of slavery continued in all societies until about a hundred years ago when the "movement for the freedom of slaves" began, because

due to the transformation of the system of human life, the issue of slaves in its old form was no longer acceptable. The abolition of slavery first began in Europe, then expanded to other countries, including America and Asia. In England, slavery continued until 1840 AD; in France until 1848; in Holland until 1863; and in America until 1865. Then, the Brussels Conference, in a declaration, decided to abolish slavery throughout the world, and this was in 1890 (meaning less than a hundred years ago).

2- The change in the form of slavery in today's world:

It is true that Westerners were the so-called pioneers in the abolition of slavery, but when we examine the issue closely, we see that slavery was not only not uprooted, but it appeared in a more dangerous and terrifying form—namely, in the form of the colonization of nations and the slavery of colonies—such that as individual slavery weakened, collective slavery and colonialism became stronger and more powerful. The British Empire, which was a pioneer in the abolition of slavery, is also considered a pioneer in the matter of colonialism!

The crimes committed by Western colonialists during their period of colonization were not only no less than the crimes of the era of slavery, but they possessed greater intensity and scope. Even after the liberation of colonies, the slavery of nations continued because this freedom was so-called political freedom, while economic and cultural colonialism still reigns in many liberated colonies and elsewhere.

Especially the communist countries, which beat their chests more than anyone else for the issue of the abolition of slavery, are themselves caught in a type of shameful general slave-holding,

and the people living in these countries, like slaves, have the least choice of their own, and everything for them is determined by the managers of the Communist Party. If someone expresses a contrary opinion, they are either sent to forced labor camps, or thrown into the dungeons of prison, or if they are among the scholars, they are sent to asylums as "mentally ill"!

In short, slavery is not subject to a name; what is ugly and unpleasant is the concept and content of slavery, and we know that this concept and content are implemented in the worst forms in colonized countries and in communist nations. The result is that the abolition of slavery in the world today has been formal and is, in truth, only a change of form!

3- The painful fate of slaves in the past

Slaves throughout history have had a very painful fate. As an example, we consider the slaves of the Spartans, who were a so-called civilized people. According to the author of The Spirit of the Laws, Spartan slaves were so wretched that they were not just the slaves of one person, but were considered slaves of the entire society, and anyone could harass and torture their own or another's slave as much as they wanted without fear of the law; in truth, their lives were worse than those of animals.

From the time slaves were hunted from underdeveloped countries until they were offered in sales markets, many of them died, and the survivors were a means for the exploitation of greedy slave-traders. The little food given to them was for survival and work, and in old age and during difficult illnesses, they were left to themselves to die in a painful manner! Therefore, the name of slavery throughout history is accompanied by a mass of horrific crimes.

With these few points clarified, we return briefly to Islam's plan regarding the gradual freedom of slaves.

4- Islam's plan for the freedom of slaves

What is often not taken into account is that if a wrong system enters the fabric of a society, uprooting it requires time, and every uncalculated movement will have a reverse result—just like a person afflicted with a dangerous disease that has fully progressed, or an addict who has been accustomed to his ugly addiction for decades. In such cases, "timed programs" must certainly be used.

To speak more clearly: if Islam had ordered the freedom of all existing slaves through a general decree, perhaps most of them would have perished, because sometimes slaves constituted half of the society. They had neither independent business nor home, nest, or means to continue life. If all were freed on a specific day and hour, a massive unemployed population would appear whose own life would be faced with danger, and who might also disrupt the order of society and launch attacks everywhere when deprivation pressured them, leading to conflict and bloodshed.

It is here that they must be freed gradually and absorbed into society, so that neither their own lives are endangered nor do they endanger the security of society. Islam followed exactly this calculated program. This program has many articles, the main points of which are presented here briefly and in a list, and their explanation requires an independent book:

Article One: Closing the sources of slavery

Slavery throughout history has had many causes. Not only were prisoners of war and debtors who lacked the power to pay their debts turned into slaves, but force and dominance were also justifications for taking and keeping slaves. Powerful countries sent their men with various weapons to underdeveloped African nations and the like, taking and capturing them in groups and taking them by ships to the markets of Asian and European countries.

Islam stopped all these matters. It allowed taking slaves in only one case, and that was regarding prisoners of war; even that was not mandatory, and as we said in the commentary of the above verses, it allowed them to be freed unconditionally or after paying a ransom according to the interests.

In those days, there were no prisons where prisoners of war could be kept until their situation was clarified, and there was no way but to distribute them among families and keep them as slaves. Obviously, when such conditions change, there is no reason for the leader of the Muslims to accept the ruling of slavery regarding captives; he can free them through "favor" (mann) and "ransom" (fida'), because Islam has given the leader of the Muslims a choice in this matter to act considering the interests. In this way, almost all new sources of slavery have been closed in Islam.

Article Two: Opening the window of freedom

Islam has organized an extensive program for the liberation of slaves which, if Muslims had acted upon it, all slaves would have been gradually

freed and absorbed into Islamic society in a not-so-long period. The main points of this program are as follows:

A- One of the eight expenditures of Zakat in Islam is buying slaves and freeing them (At-Tawbah, verse 60). In this way, a permanent and continuous budget has been considered in the Islamic treasury for this matter, which will continue until the complete freedom of slaves.

B- To complete this purpose, regulations were established in Islam so that slaves, according to a contract they make with their owner, can be freed through their own earnings (in Islamic jurisprudence, a chapter has appeared in this regard under the title of mukatabah) [^1].

C- Freeing slaves is one of the most important acts of worship and good deeds in Islam, and the leaders of Islam were pioneers in this matter, to the extent that it is written in the accounts of Ali عليه السلام: "He freed a thousand slaves from his own hand-earned labor" (اعتق الفا من كديده) [^2]!

D- The leaders of Islam would free slaves at the slightest excuse to be a model for others, to the point that when one of the slaves of Imam Baqir عليه السلام performed a good deed, the Imam عليه السلام said: "Go, for you are free, for I dislike to employ a man from the people of Paradise" (اذهب فانت حر فانى اكره ان استخدم رجلا من اهل الجنة) [^3]. In the accounts of Imam Sajjad, Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is mentioned: "His servant was pouring water on the Imam's head; the water vessel fell and injured the Imam عليه السلام. The Imam عليه السلام raised His head; the servant said: '...and those who restrain anger.' The Imam said: 'I have restrained my anger.' He said: '...and those who pardon people.' He said: 'May Allah forgive you.' He said:

'...and Allah loves the doers of good.' He said: 'Go, for you are free for the sake of Allah'" [^1].

E- In some Islamic narrations, it is mentioned: Slaves are automatically freed after seven years, as we read from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: "Whoever is a believer is freed after seven years, whether his owner frees him or not; and it is not permissible to employ one who is a believer after seven years" (من كان مؤمنا فقد عتق بعد سبع سنين، اعتقه صاحبه ام لم يعتقه، و لا يحل خدمة من كان مؤمنا بعد سبعة سنين) [^2]. F- Whoever frees a shared slave in proportion to his own share is obliged to buy the rest and free them [^4]. And whenever he frees a part of a slave of whom he is the sole owner, this freedom spreads and automatically all will be freed [^5]!

G- Whenever someone becomes the owner of his father, mother, ancestors, children, paternal uncle, paternal aunt, maternal uncle, maternal aunt, brother, sister, nephew, or niece, they are immediately freed.

Article Three: Restoring the personality of slaves

During the transitional period when slaves are traversing their path toward freedom according to Islam's calculated program, Islam has taken extensive measures to restore their rights and has revived their human personality, to the point that it makes no difference between slaves and free individuals in terms of human personality and sets piety as the criterion of value. Therefore, it allows slaves to hold all kinds of important social positions, to the point that slaves can hold the important position of judgeship [^2]. In the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as well, prominent positions ranging from army command to

other sensitive posts were entrusted to slaves or freed slaves. Many of the great companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were slaves or freed slaves, and in truth, many of them performed duties as deputies for the greats of Islam. Salman, Bilal, Ammar Yasir, and Qanbar can be named in this group. After the expedition of Bani al-Mustaliq, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله married one of the freed slave-women of this tribe, and this very act became a pretext for the freedom of all the captives of the tribe.

Article Four: Humane behavior with slaves

In Islam, many instructions have been issued regarding kindness and leniency with slaves, to the point that they have been made partners and participants in the lives of their owners. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله used to say: "Whoever has his brother under his hand must feed him from what he eats and clothe him from what he wears, and not task him beyond his ability" [^1]. Ali عليه السلام used to say to His servant Qanbar: "I am ashamed before my Lord to wear a garment better than yours, for the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله used to say: 'Clothe them from what you yourselves wear and feed them from what you yourselves eat'" [^2]. Imam Sadiq عليه السلام says: "When my father would command a servant to perform a task, he would observe; if it was a heavy task, he would say 'Bismillah' and enter into the action himself and help them" [^3]. Islam's good treatment of slaves during this transitional period was to such an extent

It is a matter that even non-Muslims have emphasized and praised.

For instance, Jurji Zaydan, in his History of Islamic Civilization, states:

"Islam is exceptionally kind to slaves. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله gave many recommendations regarding slaves, including his saying: 'Do not assign a slave a task beyond his endurance, and give him to eat of what you yourselves eat.'"

Elsewhere he says: "Do not call your servants 'slave-girl' or 'slave-boy,' but rather address them as 'my son' and 'my daughter'! The Quran also makes a remarkable recommendation regarding slaves, saying: 'Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him, and to parents do good, and to relatives, orphans, the needy, the near neighbor, the farther neighbor, the companion at your side, the traveler, and those whom your right hands possess. Indeed, Allah does not like those who are self-deluding and boastful.'" [^1]

Article Five: The Worst Act is Human Trafficking!

Fundamentally, in Islam, the buying and selling of slaves is one of the most loathsome transactions, to the extent that it is mentioned in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

شر الناس من باع الناس!

"The worst of people is the one who sells human beings!" [^2] This expression alone is sufficient to clarify Islam's ultimate view regarding slaves and to show the direction in which Islamic programs are oriented.

Even more remarkable than this is that one of the unforgivable sins in Islam is stripping humans of their freedom and liberty and turning them into a commodity, as stated in a Hadith from the Honorable Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

ان اللَّه تعالى غافر كل ذنب الا من جحد مهرا، او اغتصب اجيرا اجره، او باع رجلا حرا:

"Indeed, Allah the Almighty forgives every sin except (three sins): one who denies the dower (Mahr) of his wife, or one who usurps the wage of a laborer, or one who sells a free person!" [^1] According to this Hadith, usurping the rights of women, the rights of laborers, and stripping humans of their freedom are three unforgivable sins.

As we mentioned above, Islam allows taking slaves in only one instance, and that is in the case of prisoners of war, and even then, it is never mandatory. This is while during the era of the emergence of Islam, and for centuries thereafter, enslavement through force and attacks on the lands of Black people, capturing free humans and turning them into slaves, was very prevalent. Sometimes transactions were conducted on a horrific scale, such that in the late 18th century, the British government traded two hundred thousand slaves annually, and every year one hundred thousand people were taken from Africa and transported to America as slaves. [^2] In short, those who criticize Islam's program regarding slaves have only heard something from afar and lack precise knowledge of the principles of this program and its orientation—which is the "gradual and damage-free liberation of slaves"—or they have been influenced by biased individuals who, in their own imagination, consider this a major weak point for Islam and have launched extensive propaganda regarding it.

As for the faithless, their lot will be to fall [into ruin], and He will render their works fruitless. (8)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٩)

Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? Allah destroyed them, and a similar [fate] awaits these faithless. (10)

Commentary

If You Help Allah, He Will Help You

These verses continue to encourage the believers regarding the issue of battling the enemies of Truth. With a beautiful and eloquent expression, He motivates them toward "Jihad," stating: "O you who have believed, if you support Allah, He will support you and plant your feet firmly" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَنْصُرُوا اللَّهَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ).

The emphasis on "faith" (iman) indicates that one of the signs of true faith is struggling against the enemies of Truth.

The expression "helping Allah" clearly means helping His religion, helping His Prophet, and His Sharia and teachings. Therefore, in some other verses of the Quran, helping Allah and His Messenger are mentioned alongside each other. In Surah Al-Hashr, verse 8, we read: وَيَنْصُرُونَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ (and they support Allah and His Messenger. Those are the truthful). Although Allah's power is infinite and the power of creatures is insignificant before Him, He still uses the expression "helping Allah" to clarify the importance of Jihad and defending the religion of Truth; no expression more magnificent than this could be found for this subject.

But let us see what promise Allah has given to the mujahideen in exchange for defending His religion. First, He says: "He will support you." But through which means?

Through many ways: He casts the light of faith into your hearts, piety into your souls, strength into your will, and tranquility into your thoughts.

On the other hand, He sends angels to aid you, changes the course of events in your favor, inclines the hearts of people toward you, makes your words influential, and renders your activities fruitful. Yes, Allah's help encompasses the body and soul, the internal and the external.

However, among all forms of assistance, He emphasizes "firmness of feet" (steadfastness), because standing firm against the enemy is the most important secret to victory, and the winners of wars are those who show greater stability and endurance.

Thus, in the story of the battle of "Talut" (Saul), the great commander of the Israelites, against "Jalut" (Goliath), that tyrannical, bloodthirsty, and powerful ruler, we read that the few believers who were with him, when they faced the massive enemy host, said:

"رَبَّنَا أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَانْصُرْنَا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكَافِرِينَ": "Our Lord, pour upon us patience and plant firmly our feet and give us victory over the disbelieving people."

And in the following verse, we read: فَهَزَمُوهُمْ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ, "So they defeated them by permission of Allah." Yes, the result of steadfastness is victory over the enemy.

Since sometimes the concentrated force of the enemy, their vast numbers, and various equipment preoccupy the minds of the mujahideen of the path of Truth, in the next verse He adds: "But those who disbelieve—for them is misery and He will waste their deeds" (وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْسًا لَهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَالَهُمْ) 1. "Ta's" (rhyming with nahs) means slipping and falling on one's face, and the fact that some have interpreted it as "destruction and degradation" is actually its necessary consequence.

In any case, the contrast between these two is very meaningful. Regarding the true believers, He says: "He plants their feet firmly," but regarding the disbelievers, He says: "May slipping and falling be upon them," and this is in the form of a curse, which is more impactful and expressive.

Yes, when faithless individuals slip, there is no one to take them by the arm, and they will easily fall into precipices. But the angels of divine mercy hasten to the aid of the believers and preserve them in slippery places and pitfalls, as we read elsewhere: "Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remained on a right course—the angels will descend upon them" (Fussilat: 30).

The deeds of the believers are blessed, but the deeds of the disbelievers are devoid of blessing and are quickly erased and lost.

The next verse explains the reason for their fall and the destruction of their deeds as follows: "That is because they disliked what Allah revealed, so He rendered their deeds worthless" (ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَالَهُمْ). Allah revealed, above all else, the creed of Monotheism (Tawhid), but they turned their backs on it and turned toward polytheism. Allah commanded truth, justice, purity, and piety, but they

1: "Ta'san" is an absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq) for a hidden verb, estimated as ta'sahum ta'san, and the phrase "adalla a'malahum" is conjoined to this hidden verb. Both are in the form of a curse, like "qatalahum Allah," and it is clear that a curse from Allah means its occurrence.

cast them all behind them and turned toward oppression and corruption. They even expressed detestation when the name of the One God was mentioned: "And when Allah is mentioned alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter shrink with aversion" (Az-Zumar: 45).

Yes, since they hated these matters, they naturally took no steps in this path, and all their efforts and endeavors were in the path of falsehood. It is natural that such deeds should be rendered worthless and destroyed.

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated: "They disliked what Allah revealed regarding the right of Ali." They disliked what Allah had revealed concerning the right of Ali عليه السلام 1. Of course, the expression "what Allah revealed" has a broad meaning, of which one clear instance is the issue of the Wilayah (guardianship) of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, not that it is exclusively limited to it.

Since the Holy Quran often presents physical examples to rebellious oppressors, here too it invites them to study the states of past nations, saying: "Have they not traveled through the land and seen how was the end of those before them? Allah destroyed [everything] over them" (أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ دَمَّرَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ). Then, so they do not think the painful fate of previous rebellious nations was an exceptional case, He adds: "and for the disbelievers is something comparable" (وَلِلْكَافِرِينَ أَمْثَالُهَا) [2]. They should not expect that by performing similar deeds, they will remain immune and exempt from similar punishments. Let them go and look at the remains of those who passed and see their own future in the mirror of their lives.

It is noteworthy that "dammara" from the root tadmir originally means to destroy, but when mentioned with "ala" (over), it means destroying everything, even the children, family, and specific wealth of a person 1. Thus, this expression describes a more painful calamity, especially considering that the word "ala" is usually used for dominance; the meaning of the sentence becomes that Allah poured destruction and annihilation upon these nations, their wealth, and all that was dear to them!

Regarding "traveling through the land" (sayr fil-ard), which the Holy Quran emphasizes many times as an enlightening program, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 3, page 102 onwards (under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran) and in Volume 16, page 458.

The last verse under discussion mentions the reason for the Lord's comprehensive support for the believers and the destruction of the rebellious disbelievers, saying: "That is because Allah is the protector of those who have believed and because the disbelievers have no protector" (ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكَافِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَى لَهُمْ) [2]. "Mawla" means "guardian, protector, and helper." Thus, Allah has taken upon Himself the guardianship, protection, and assistance of the believers, while He has removed the disbelievers from under the umbrella of His guardianship. It is clear that those under the guardianship of His Pure Essence will be helped in difficulties, will have firm feet, and will ultimately reach their goal. But those who are outside this covering, their deeds are wasted and destroyed,

[2]: The demonstrative pronoun "dhalika" (that) refers to the good end of the believers and the bad end of the disbelievers mentioned in the previous verses.

and the end of their affair is destruction.

A question arises here: in the verse under discussion, Allah is mentioned only as the Mawla of the believers, whereas in some other verses of the Quran, such as verse 30 of Surah Yunus, He is introduced even as the Mawla of the disbelievers: "And they will be returned to Allah, their true Mawla, and lost from them is whatever they used to invent."

The answer to this question becomes clear with one point: the general guardianship (Wilayah) of Allah, which is the matter of His creation and providence, encompasses everyone. However, His special guardianship and specific grace, which are accompanied by various forms of support, only include the believers 1. Some have said this verse is the most hopeful verse in the Quran ("arja ayatin fil-Quran") because it introduces all believers—whether scholar or ignorant, ascetic or worldly, small or great, man or woman, young or old—as being under the special protection and grace of the Lord. It does not even exclude sinful believers. In harsh events and soul-crushing calamities, He shows examples of His support, which everyone has felt throughout their life, and there are numerous evidences in history.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting alone under a tree after one of the battles. A polytheist with a drawn sword attacked the Prophet by surprise and said: "Who can save you from me now?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Allah." At that moment, the polytheist's foot slipped and he fell to the ground, and the sword fell from his hand. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله picked up the sword and said: "Now you tell me, who will release you from my hand?"

He replied: "No one!" and then he embraced Islam 1. Yes, He is the Mawla of all believers, and the disbelievers have no Mawla or refuge.

1: Some, like Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," have interpreted "Mawla" in the verse under discussion as "Helper" and in the verse of Surah Yunus and its like as "Owner."

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ وَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ (١٢)
Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. As for the faithless, they enjoy and eat like the cattle do, and the Fire will be their [final] abode. (12)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ هِىَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِّن قَرْيَتِكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَا نَاصِرَ لَهُمْ (١٣)
How many a town We have destroyed which was more powerful than your town which expelled you, and they had no helper. (13)
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ كَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم (١٤)
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their conduct is made to seem decorous and who follow their desires? (14)

Commentary

The Fate of the Believers and the Disbelievers

Since the preceding verses spoke of the continuous struggle between Truth and Falsehood, and Faith and Disbelief, the verses under discussion explain the fate of the believers and the disbelievers in a clear comparison. This is to clarify that these two groups are not only different in the life of this world but are also profoundly different in the Hereafter. It states: "Indeed, Allah will admit those who believe and do righteous deeds into gardens [of Paradise] beneath which rivers flow" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

"While those who disbelieve enjoy themselves [with the fleeting pleasures of this world] and eat as cattle eat, and the Fire will be their residence!" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَ يَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ وَ النَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ) [1]. It is true that both groups live in this world and benefit from its blessings, but the difference lies here: the goal of the believers is to perform righteous deeds—deeds that are beneficial, constructive, and aimed at seeking the pleasure of the Lord.

However, the entire goal of the disbelievers is merely this eating, sleeping, and enjoying the pleasures of life.

Believers move with awareness, while disbelievers live without a purpose and die without a purpose, exactly like cattle! Believers observe many conditions and limits in utilizing the blessings of life; they think deeply about the legitimacy, the means of acquisition, and the manner of consumption. But the disbelievers are like cattle; for them, it makes no difference whether this grass belongs to their owner or is usurped. Is it the right of an orphan or a widow, or not?

Believers, while using any of the material blessings, think of the Bestower of these blessings; they see His signs in them and fulfill the duty of gratitude to the Benefactor. Meanwhile, the heedless and ignorant disbeliever thinks of none of these things and continuously makes his burden heavier with oppression and sin, bringing himself closer to destruction—just as fattened sheep, the more they eat and grow fat, the closer they get to slaughter.

Some have described the difference between these two groups as follows: "The believer's eating is never devoid of three things: piety (wara‘) when seeking, observing etiquette (adab) when using, and eating for a purpose (sabab). But the disbeliever seeks out of greed, eats for lust, and his life is entirely in heedlessness."

It is noteworthy that regarding the believers, He says Allah admits them into the gardens of Paradise, but regarding the disbelievers, He says: "the Fire will be their residence." The first expression is one of honor and regard for the status of the people of faith, while the second expression is one of humiliation and disregard for the disbelievers who have left His guardianship (Wilayah).

Some commentators have inferred from the phrase "وَ النَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ" (the Fire is their residence) that they are in the Fire even now, because the sentence is not in the present or future tense but reports a current state. In reality, it is so, for their deeds and thoughts are themselves the fire in which they are trapped, and Hell already surrounds them from every side, even though these animal-like beings are heedless. As we read in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 49: "And indeed, Hell is encompassing the disbelievers" (وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ).

In several other verses of the Quran, these denizens of Hell are likened to cattle, or rather, worse than cattle: "Those are like cattle; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless" (Al-A'raf, 179), the detailed explanation of which we provided in Volume 7, page 19 onwards.

In the next verse, to complete this objective, He makes a comparison between the polytheists of Mecca and the previous idolaters, threatening them severely with an eloquent expression. Meanwhile, He emphasizes some of their great crimes which serve as justification for fighting them, saying: "And how many a city was stronger in power than your city which drove you out? We destroyed them, and there was no helper for them" (وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ هِيَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِنْ قَرْيَتِكَ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ فَلا ناصِرَ لَهُمْ).

They should not imagine that because the world goes their way for a few days, and they have become so bold as to drive out the greatest Divine Messenger from the holiest of cities, that this situation will continue forever. Compared to the people of 'Ad, Thamud, the Pharaohs, and the army of Abrahah, these are weak and helpless beings. The God who crushed them easily finds it very simple to break these people as well.

In a narration from Ibn Abbas, it is stated that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله went from Mecca toward the cave (Thawr), he turned his face back toward Mecca and said:

"You are the most beloved of lands to Allah, and you are the most beloved of lands to me; and had your polytheist inhabitants not driven me out, I would not have left you by my own will."

At this point, the above verse was revealed (giving the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله the glad tidings of Divine victory and threatening the enemies with punishment) [1]. According to this occasion of revelation, this verse was revealed in Mecca. However, it seems that this occasion of revelation pertains to verse 85 of Surah Al-Qasas, which many commentators have mentioned there, and it bears more relevance to that verse, which says:

"Indeed, He who imposed the Quran upon you will take you back to a place of return [your birthplace]" (إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى‌ مَعادٍ) [2]. It is noteworthy that here, the act of "driving out" is attributed to the city of Mecca, while the people of the city are intended. This is a subtle metaphor that identifies the authority of a group over that city, and similar instances occur elsewhere in the Holy Quran. Furthermore, the term "qaryah" (village/town), as we have said many times, applies to any city or inhabited area and does not exclusively mean a rural village.

The last verse under discussion speaks of another comparison between the "believers" and the "disbelievers"—two groups different in everything: one possessing faith and righteous deeds, and the other living a life that is animalistic in every sense; two groups, one under the umbrella of the Lord's guardianship, and the other without a master or protector. It says:

"Is he who is on a clear proof from his Lord like him for whom the evil of his work has been made attractive and they follow their [own] desires?" (أَ فَمَنْ كانَ عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَ اتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ).

The first group, in their path, enjoys correct recognition, certainty, and definitive proof and evidence. They see their "path" and "goal" clearly and advance rapidly. But the second group is afflicted with poor judgment, a lack of understanding of reality, and darkness of path and goal; they wander in the shadows of delusions. The cause of this wandering is following rebellious whims and desires, for whims and desires cast a veil over human intellect and thought, making the ugly appear beautiful and the beautiful appear ugly, to the extent that they sometimes boast and pride themselves on their shameful and disgraceful acts. As stated in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 103: "Say, [O Muhammad], 'Shall we [believers] inform you of the greatest losers as to [their] deeds? [They are] those whose effort is lost in worldly life, while they think that they are doing well in work.' Those are the ones who disbelieve in the verses of their Lord and in [their] meeting Him, so their deeds have become worthless, and We will not assign to them on the Day of Resurrection any weight" (because they have no deeds of substance).

"Bayyinah" means clear proof, and here it refers to the Quran, the miracles of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and other rational proofs.

It is clear that the interrogation in the sentence "أَ فَمَنْ كانَ..." is a "rhetorical question of denial," meaning these two groups are never equal.

Regarding who makes the evil deeds of the desire-followers appear attractive to them—is it Allah, or themselves, or the devils? It must be said: all of these are correct, as it has been attributed to all three in the verses of the Quran. In Surah An-Naml, verse 4, He says: "Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter—We have made their deeds pleasing to them" (إِنَّ الَّذِي لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ). And in several verses, including Al-Ankabut 38, it says: "And Satan made their deeds pleasing to them" (وَ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ). In the verse under discussion, given the phrase "and they follow their [own] desires," it appears that this beautification stems from following whims and desires. This meaning is fully understandable, as whims and desires strip a person of the sense of discernment and the correct understanding of realities.

Of course, attributing it to "Satan" is also correct, as he fans the flames of whims and desires and constantly whispers to man.

And if it is attributed to Allah, it is because He is the "Cause of Causes," and every cause has an effect that originates from Him. He gave fire its burning quality, and gave whims and desires the effect of veiling the truth, and He had previously announced this effect. Thus, the primary root of responsibility returns to the human being himself.

Some consider the phrase "فَمَنْ كانَ عَلى‌ بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ" to refer to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the subsequent phrase to refer to the disbelievers of Mecca, but it appears that the verse has a broad meaning of which these are specific instances.

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6055. [2]: For more explanation on this matter, please refer to Vol. 16, p. 184 onwards.

Ayah

مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ فِيهَآ أَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن مَّآءٍ غَيْرِ ءَاسِنٍ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن لَّبَنٍ لَّمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُۥ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ خَمْرٍ لَّذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ عَسَلٍ مُّصَفًّى ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ كَمَنْ هُوَ خَـٰلِدٌ فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَسُقُوا۟ مَآءً حَمِيمًا فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعَآءَهُمْ (١٥)
A description of the paradise promised to the Godwary: therein are streams of unstaling water and streams of milk unchanging in flavour, and streams of wine delicious to the drinkers, and streams of purified honey; there will be every kind of fruit in it for them, and forgiveness from their Lord. [Are such ones] like those who abide in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water which cuts up their bowels? (15)

Commentary

Another Description of Paradise

This verse continues the description of the fates of two groups: the disbelievers and the believers—one possessing righteous deeds and the other possessing ugly and shameful acts that have been made to seem fair-seeming to them.

In this verse, six types of blessings for the people of Paradise and two types of severe and painful punishments for the people of Hell are unveiled, defining the ultimate end of these two groups.

Among the blessings of the people of Paradise, it mentions four rivers, each containing a specific liquid, followed by a mention of heavenly fruits, and finally, it speaks of certain spiritual blessings.

First, He says: "The description of the Paradise promised to the righteous is that in it are rivers of water, pure and clear, whose smell has not changed" (مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ فِيها أَنْهارٌ مِنْ ماءٍ غَيْرِ آسِنٍ) [^1]. "Asin" (آسن) means "foul-smelling"; therefore, "ماءٍ غَيْرِ آسِنٍ" refers to water whose smell has not altered due to stagnation or any other cause.
