This is the first category of heavenly rivers, in which only crystal-clear water with a pleasant scent and taste flows.

Then He adds: "And rivers of milk whose taste has not changed" (وَ أَنْهارٌ مِنْ لَبَنٍ لَمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُ).

Fundamentally, that realm is not a place of decay or corruption, and heavenly food items do not change over time. It is this worldly realm where, due to the existence of various destructive microbes, food items rapidly move toward corruption.

Next, moving to the third category of heavenly rivers, He says: "And rivers of pure wine, a delight to those who drink!" (وَ أَنْهارٌ مِنْ خَمْرٍ لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ).

And finally, He describes the fourth and last category of heavenly rivers in this manner: "And rivers of purified honey" (وَ أَنْهارٌ مِنْ عَسَلٍ مُصَفًّى).

In addition to these various rivers, each created for a specific purpose, the fifth blessing mentions various types of heavenly fruits, adding: "And for them therein are all kinds of fruits" (وَ لَهُمْ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ) [^1]. Colorful fruits with different tastes and fragrances—whatever can be imagined, or types that are currently beyond our imagination—are all at their disposal.

And finally, in the sixth blessing, which—unlike the previous material blessings—has a spiritual and psychological aspect, He says: "And forgiveness from their Lord" (وَ مَغْفِرَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ).

A vast and expansive mercy that covers all their slips, grants them certainty and tranquility, and makes them beloved in the presence of the Truth. They become the manifestation of: "Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him; that is the great success" (رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ) (Al-Ma'idah: 119).

Thus, the pure-hearted and righteous believers enjoy various material and spiritual blessings in the eternal Paradise, in the proximity of Divine mercy.

Now let us see what fate awaits the opposing group. In the continuation of the verse, He says:

"Are [these people] like those who abide eternally in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water that cuts their intestines to pieces?"

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical structure of this noble verse, commentators have many discussions. The most appropriate is to say: "Mathalul-Jannah" (The description of Paradise) is the subject (mubtada) and its predicate (khabar) is omitted, estimated as: "The description of the Paradise promised to the righteous is [that of] a Paradise in which there are rivers..." In fact, this verse is similar to verse 35 of Surah Ar-Ra'd, which says: "The description of the Paradise promised to the righteous is [that] rivers flow beneath it."

[^1]: There is an omitted phrase here, estimated as: "For them therein are varieties of every kind of fruit."

(كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ فِي النَّارِ وَ سُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ) [^1]. "Am'a" (امعاء) is the plural of "ma'y" (rhyming with sa'y) or "ma'a" (rhyming with ghina), meaning intestine, and sometimes it refers to everything inside the abdomen. Their being torn apart indicates the extreme scalding nature and heat of this terrifying drink of Hell.

Key Points

1. The Four Heavenly Rivers

It is clearly understood from the verses of the Holy Quran that in Paradise there are various rivers and springs, each possessing a specific benefit and pleasure. Four examples are mentioned in the above verse, and other examples are found in Surah Al-Dahr (Al-Insan), which, God willing, will be discussed in its commentary.

The use of the plural "rivers" (anhar) regarding these four types shows that there is not just one river of each, but rather "rivers."

We have said many times that heavenly blessings are not things that can be fully described using the everyday vocabulary of this world. These words are too small to provide a complete and vivid depiction; rather, they can only draw a faint shadow of those great realities in our minds.

In the verse under discussion, rivers of "water," "milk," "pure wine," and "honey" are mentioned. It is possible that the first is for quenching thirst, the second for nourishment, the third for exhilaration, and the fourth for pleasure and strength.

[^1]: Again, there has been much discussion regarding the structure of this sentence. The most appropriate is that the verse has an implied meaning: "Is he who abides eternally in the Paradise whose attributes are these, like him who abides eternally in the Fire?"

Interestingly, it is understood from other Quranic verses that not all inhabitants of Paradise drink from all these beverages; rather, there are ranks and levels through which they benefit accordingly. In Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 28, we read: "A spring from which those nearest [to Allah] drink" (عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ).

2. Pure Wine (Sharaban Tahura)

It goes without saying that the wine (khamr) of Paradise has no connection to the contaminated wine of this world. As the Quran describes it elsewhere:

"No bad effect is there in it, nor from it will they be intoxicated" (لا فِيها غَوْلٌ وَ لا هُمْ عَنْها يُنْزَفُونَ). That wine is neither a cause for the corruption of the intellect nor does it lead to drunkenness; it contains nothing but alertness, joy, and spiritual pleasure (As-Saffat: 47).

3. Non-Perishable Drinks

In describing the heavenly rivers, in one instance "non-asin" (its smell has not changed) is used, and in another "its taste has not altered" (لم يتغير طعمه). This shows that heavenly drinks and foods always possess the same freshness and vitality as the first day. Why should it be otherwise? While the alteration of food substances and their change or corruption is due to the effect of spoiling microbes, if these did not exist, everything in this world would also remain in its original state. However, since there is no place for corruptive organisms in Paradise, everything there is always clear, pure, healthy, and fresh.

4. Why Fruits?

In the verse under discussion and many other verses of the Quran, among heavenly foods, the emphasis is mostly on fruits—diverse fruits that suit all tastes.

This indicates that fruit is the most important heavenly food; indeed, the best and healthiest food for humans in this world is also fruit.

5. The Expression "Suqu" (They are given to drink)

The expression "suqu" (سُقُوا - they are given to drink) in the passive voice expresses the reality that the scalding water (hamim) is forced upon them, not by their choice. Instead of quenching their thirst in that scorching fire, it disintegrates their intestines, and as is the nature of Hell, they return to their original state because there is no death there!

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا خَرَجُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مَاذَا قَالَ ءَانِفًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ (١٦)
There are some among them who prick up their ears at you. But when they go out from your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’ They are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal, and they follow their own desires. (16)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ زَادَهُمْ هُدًى وَءَاتَىٰهُمْ تَقْوَىٰهُمْ (١٧)
As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances their guidance and invests them with their Godwariness. (17)
فَهَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَ أَشْرَاطُهَا ۚ فَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمْ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ذِكْرَىٰهُمْ (١٨)
Do they await anything except that the Hour should overtake them suddenly? Its portents have already come. When it overtakes them of what avail will the admonitions they were given? (18)
فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْوَىٰكُمْ (١٩)
Know that there is no god except Allah, and plead [to Allah] for forgiveness of your sin and for the faithful, men and women. Allah knows your itinerary and your [final] abode. (19)

Commentary

The Signs of the Resurrection Have Appeared!

These verses depict the state of the hypocrites in their encounter with Divine revelation, the verses and words of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and the issue of war and struggle against the enemies of Islam.

In the Medinan Surahs, there is much discussion about the hypocrites, whereas there is no mention of them in the Meccan Surahs. This is because the issue of hypocrisy arose after the victory of Islam and its attainment of power and authority; the opponents found themselves in a weaker position such that they could no longer openly express their opposition. Therefore, they outwardly donned the garment of Islam to remain safe from the wrath of the true Muslims, while inwardly they were busy with various conspiracies. The Jews of Medina, who possessed considerable military and economic power, were also considered a support for the hypocrites.

In any case, they took their place in the ranks of the true believers and participated in the Friday prayers or in the presence of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), but their reactions to the verses of the Quran revealed their diseased hearts.

In the first verse under discussion, it says: "Among them are those who listen to you, but when they leave your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge—out of mockery and contempt—'What did this man just say?'" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّى إِذا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ ما ذا قالَ آنِفاً).

By "this man," they meant the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

Their expression regarding the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and his profound words was so ugly, offensive, and derogatory that it showed they had not believed in the heavenly revelation at all.

"Anifan" (آنفاً) is derived from the root "anf" (انف), meaning "nose." Since the nose has a particular prominence on the human face, this word is used for the noble individuals of a tribe, and it is also used for a time immediately preceding the present, as it appears in the verse under discussion.

Furthermore, the expression "to those who have been given knowledge" (لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ) shows that one of the characteristics of the believers is possessing sufficient awareness. Indeed, it is knowledge that is the source of faith, as well as its fruit and product.

However, at the end of the verse, the Quran gives them a crushing response, saying:

"The words of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) are not incomprehensible nor do they have any particular complexity; rather, they are those upon whose hearts Allah has set a seal and who have followed their own desires, so they understand nothing of it!" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طبعَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَ اتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ‌).

In reality, the second phrase is a cause for the first; that is, the worship of desires strips a person of the power to perceive truths and the sense of discernment, and casts a veil over his heart, such that the hearts of the desire-worshippers become like a vessel that has been closed and sealed—nothing enters it and nothing leaves it.

In contrast to them are the true believers, about whom the next verse says: "As for those who are guided, He increases them in guidance and grants them their piety" (وَ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً وَ آتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ‌).

Yes, they personally took the first steps of guidance and utilized their intellect, reason, and fitrah (innate nature) in this path; then Allah, according to the promise He has given, guides and directs the strivers in His path further, casts the light of faith into their hearts, and grants them expansion of the chest and insight. This is from the perspective of belief and faith; as for action, He enlivens the spirit of piety (taqwa) within them, such that they loathe sin and love obedience and goodness.

In both aspects, they are the exact opposite of the hypocrites mentioned in the previous verse: on one hand, the hearts of the hypocrites are sealed and they understand nothing, and on the other hand, in action, they always follow their whims. But the believers increase day by day in their guidance and, in the realm of action, in their piety.

The next verse serves as a warning to this faithless, mocking group, saying: "Do they wait for anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly? For its signs have already come. But how will their reminder benefit them when it has actually arrived?" (فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً فَقَدْ جاءَ أَشْراطُها فَأَنَّى لَهُمْ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ ذِكْراهُمْ‌).

Indeed, at the time when they should believe and it would be beneficial, they show stubbornness and persistence in falsehood and do not submit to the truth; rather, they rise to ridicule and mockery. But when the terrifying events and the beginning of the Resurrection shake the world, such individuals will fall into terror and express humility and faith, while it will be of no benefit to them.

This expression is exactly like saying to someone: "Are you waiting to bring a doctor and medicine when the matter is finished and your patient is on the verge of death?" So come, before the opportunity is lost, take a step—a beneficial and effective step.

"Ashrat" (اشراط) is the plural of "sharat" (on the pattern of sharaf), meaning "sign." Therefore, "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" refers to the signs of the approach of the Resurrection.

Regarding what is meant by the signs of the approach of the Resurrection here, commentators have many discussions, and even brief and detailed treatises have been written on this subject.

However, many believe that "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" in the verse under discussion refers to the rising of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) himself, as evidenced by a hadith narrated from him, where he said:

"I was sent, and the Hour is like these two," and he joined his index and middle fingers together. [^1] Some have also counted the "Splitting of the Moon" and certain other events of the Prophet's era as part of the "Ashrat al-Sa'ah."

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Fi Zilal, and other commentaries under the verses discussed (with slight differences in wording).

Numerous hadiths have also been narrated in this regard, specifically identifying the widespread prevalence of many sins among the people as signs of the approach of the Resurrection.

For example, a hadith narrated by Sheikh Mufid in Rawdat al-Wa'izin from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) states:

"Among the signs of the Hour are that knowledge will be taken away, ignorance will appear, wine will be drunk, and adultery will become rampant." [^1] Even significant and impactful events such as the uprising of Imam Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him) have been counted as "Ashrat al-Sa'ah."

However, it is necessary to remind that sometimes we discuss "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" in an absolute sense—what are the signs of the approach of the Resurrection?—and sometimes specifically regarding this verse.

Regarding the verse, the matter is as we have stated. But concerning the signs of the approach of the Resurrection in an absolute sense, there are many discussions and narrations found in famous Islamic books, which we will mention in the "Key Points" section. [^2]

Is the Rising of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) a Sign of the Nearness of the Resurrection?

Here the question arises: how can the rising of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) be counted as

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 37.

[^2]: Based on what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of the phrase "its signs have already come" is not that all the signs of the Resurrection appeared in the era of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), but rather that some of them appeared, which herald the nearness of the Resurrection, even if parts of it appear later.

one of the signs of the nearness of the Resurrection, while 15 centuries have passed and there is still no sign of the Resurrection?

The answer to this question becomes clear by considering one point: the remaining duration of the world must be calculated in comparison to its past. In such a comparison, what remains of the world's life is not significant. As stated in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), one day after the afternoon prayer and near sunset, he was delivering a sermon to his companions and said:

"By Him in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family), what has passed of the world compared to what remains of it is only like what has passed of this day of yours compared to what remains of it, and what remains of it is but a little." [^1]

The last verse under discussion, as a conclusion to the dialogues in the previous verses regarding faith, disbelief, and the fate of the believers and disbelievers, says: "So know that there is no deity except Allah" (فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ‌).

That is, stand firm on the line of monotheism (Tawhid), for the healing medicine and the best means of salvation is this very monotheism, the effects of which were explained in the previous verses.

Therefore, the meaning of this statement is not that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was unaware of monotheism; rather, the intent is the continuation of this path, exactly like what has been said in the commentary of Surah al-Fatihah regarding the verse "Guide us to the straight path," where the meaning is not a lack of guidance, but rather "keep us firm on the path of guidance."

[^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 48.

There is also the possibility that the intent is deeper reflection on the matter of monotheism and ascending to higher stations, for this is a matter where the more one reflects upon it and studies the signs of God, the more one reaches a more sublime stage. Analyzing what was said in the previous verses regarding faith and disbelief is itself a factor for increasing faith.

A third interpretation is that it refers to the practical aspects of monotheism; that is, know that the only refuge in the universe is Him—seek refuge in Him, ask only Him for the solution to your problems, and never be terrified by the multitude of enemies.

These three interpretations are not contradictory and may be combined in the meaning of the verse.

Following this doctrinal issue, it returns to the matter of piety and purity from sin, adding: "And ask forgiveness for your sin and for the believing men and believing women" (واسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ‌).

It is clear that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), by virtue of the station of infallibility ('Ismah), never committed a sin. Such expressions either refer to the issue of "leaving the better" (tark al-awla) and "the good deeds of the righteous are the sins of the near ones," or they serve as a model for the Muslims.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that Hudhayfah al-Yamani said: "I was a man of sharp tongue and was harsh toward my family. I said, 'O Messenger of Allah! I fear that the consequence of my tongue will lead me to Hell!'" The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said:

"Where do you stand regarding seeking forgiveness? Indeed, I seek forgiveness from Allah one hundred times a day!" (In some narrations, seventy times is mentioned). [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 102 (under the verses discussed).

If others seek forgiveness for their sins and transgressions, the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) seeks forgiveness for that moment he was distracted from the remembrance of Allah or left a better deed to pursue a good one.

It is also noteworthy that here Allah has interceded for the believing men and women and commanded His Prophet to seek forgiveness for them so that He may include them in His mercy. From here, the depth of the issue of "intercession" (shafa'ah) in this world and the hereafter, as well as the legitimacy and importance of the issue of "recourse" (tawassul), becomes apparent.

And at the end of the verse, as a statement of the cause, He says: "And Allah knows your place of movement and your place of rest" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ‌ و مثويكم).

He is aware of your outward and manifest, your inward and hidden, your secrets and your whispers; He is even fully aware of your thoughts and intentions in your movements and stillness. For this reason, you must turn to Him and seek pardon from His threshold.

"Mutaqallab" means the place of coming and going, and "mathwa" means the place of settlement. [^1] It appears that these two words have a broad meaning that includes all movements and stillness of man, whether in this world or the hereafter, whether during the fetal period or in the graves, although many commentators have provided limited meanings for them.

Some have said it refers to man's movements during the day and his rest at night.

Others have said it refers to man's path in this world and his abode in the hereafter.

[^1]: Therefore, "mutaqallab" is a passive participle used here as a noun of place, but some consider it a "mim-infinitive" (masdar mimi), meaning the transition from one state to another. However, the first meaning is more appropriate given the parallel with "mathwa," which is certainly a noun of place.

Others have mentioned the transformation and transition of man in the loins of fathers and the wombs of mothers, and his stability in the grave.

And finally, some have cited his movements during travel and his tranquility while at home.

But as we said, the verse has a broad concept that includes all of these.

Key Point: What are the Signs of the Hour?

As we said, "Ashrat" is the plural of "sharat," meaning "sign," and "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" refers to the signs of the approach of the Resurrection, which have been discussed in many narrations in both Shia and Sunni sources, and in the Holy Quran, it is mentioned only in this instance.

One of the most detailed and comprehensive hadiths in this regard is a hadith narrated by Ibn Abbas from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) during the story of the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada'), which teaches us many things and contains numerous points. For this reason, we provide the full text below:

He says: We were with the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) during the "Farewell Pilgrimage" (the last Hajj the Prophet performed in the final year of his life). The Prophet took hold of the door ring of the Kaaba, turned to us, and said: "Shall I inform you of the signs of the Hour?"—and Salman was the closest of all to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that day—he replied, "Yes, O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family)."

He said: "Among the signs of the Hour are the neglect of prayer, the following of lusts, the inclination toward the worship of desires, the honoring of the wealthy, and the selling of religion for the world. It is at this time that the heart of the believer melts within him, just as salt melts in water, because of all the evils he sees and is unable to change."

Salman said, "Will such a thing happen, O Messenger of Allah?"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, oppressive rulers, profligate ministers, tyrannical experts, and treacherous trustees will rule over the people."

Salman asked, "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, evils will become beautiful and beauties will become evil; trust will be given to the traitor, and the trustworthy will be betrayed; the liar will be believed, and the truthful will be rejected!"

Salman asked: "Will such a thing happen, O Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! On that day, governance will be in the hands of women, and consultation will be with slaves; children will sit upon the pulpits, and lying will be considered cleverness, Zakat a penalty, and the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) a spoil!"

People will be unkind to their parents and kind to their friends, and a comet will appear in the sky.

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, the wife will become a business partner with her husband (and both will exert all their efforts outside the home to amass wealth); rain will be scarce, the generous will become stingy, and the destitute will be held in contempt. At that time, markets will draw near to one another;

one will say, 'I sold nothing,' and another will say, 'I made no profit,' and all will open their tongues in complaint and blame of the Lord!"

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, groups will come to power who, if the people speak, they kill them, and if they remain silent, they deem everything of theirs permissible; they plunder their wealth, trample their honor, and shed their blood, filling hearts with enmity and terror; you will see all the people frightened, fearful, and intimidated!"

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, something will be brought from the East and something from the West (laws from the East and laws from the West), and my Ummah will become multi-colored! Woe on that day to the weak of my Ummah from them, and woe to them from the Divine punishment; they show no mercy to the small, nor respect to the great, nor do they forgive a sinner; their bodies are like those of humans, but their hearts are the hearts of devils!"

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family)?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, men will be content with men, and women with women, and they will compete over boys just as they compete over girls in their families! Men will make themselves resemble women and women will make themselves resemble men, and women will ride upon saddles (and display themselves); may the curse of Allah be upon them!"

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family)?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At

that time, they will decorate the mosques just as the temples of the Jews and Christians are decorated; they will adorn the Qurans (without acting upon their content); the minarets of the mosques will be tall, and the ranks of worshippers numerous, but hearts will be hostile toward one another and tongues will be diverse!"

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, the boys of my Ummah will be adorned with gold, and they will wear silk, brocade, and fine fabrics, and they will make clothes for themselves from leopard skins!"

Salman said: "Is this thing to happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, adultery will become manifest, transactions will be conducted with backbiting and bribery, they will abandon religion and take up the world."

Salman said: "Is this a thing that will happen, O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family)?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, divorce will increase, and no legal punishment (Hadd) of Allah will be implemented, yet despite this, they do not harm Allah (they only harm themselves)."

Salman said: "Is this a thing that will happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, female singers and instruments of idle play and music will appear, and the wicked of my Ummah will follow them."

Salman said: "Is this to happen, O Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, the wealthy of my Ummah will go for Hajj for recreation, the middle class for trade,

and the poor among them for hypocrisy and showing off! At that time, groups will emerge who learn the Quran for other than Allah and treat it like musical instruments; and groups will come to power who learn the knowledge of religion for other than Allah; illegitimate children will be numerous, they will recite the Quran in a song-like manner (ghina), and they will race one another for the world."

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! This is at a time when the veils of sanctity are torn, sin is abundant, the wicked prevail over the good, lying increases, stubbornness becomes manifest, and poverty grows; people will boast to one another with various types of clothing, unseasonal rains will fall, they will consider gambling and musical instruments attractive, and they will deem commanding the right and forbidding the wrong as ugly.

To the extent that the believer at that time will be the most humiliated of all the Ummah; the reciters of the Quran and the worshippers will constantly slander one another, and they will be called filthy and polluted individuals in the kingdom of the heavens."

Salman said: "Will this happen, O Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family)?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, the rich will show no mercy to the poor, to the point that a needy person will stand up among the crowd and express his need, and no one will place anything in his hand!"

Salman said: "Is this thing to happen, O Messenger of Allah?!"

He said: "Yes, by Him in whose hand is my soul, O Salman! At that time, the 'Ruwaybidah' will speak!"

Salman said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! What is the 'Ruwaybidah'?"

He said: "One will speak regarding the deprived who never used to speak (and someone will express an opinion who was never given the chance to express an opinion)."

At this time, it will not be long before a cry rises from the earth, such that every group will imagine this cry is in their own region.

They will remain in that state for as long as Allah wills, then during this period, the earth will split open and the earth will cast out the pieces of its heart; he said: I mean gold and silver. Then he pointed to the pillars of the mosque with his blessed hand and said: Like these! And on that day, gold and silver will no longer be of any use (and the Divine command will arrive). This is the meaning of the Lord's words: 'For its signs have already come.'" [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn and Tafsir al-Safi (under the verse discussed).

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ ۖ فَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُّحْكَمَةٌ وَذُكِرَ فِيهَا ٱلْقِتَالُ ۙ رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ ٱلْمَغْشِىِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٠)
The faithful say, ‘If only a surah were sent down!’ But when a conclusive surah is sent down and war is mentioned in it, you see those in whose hearts is a sickness looking upon you with the look of someone fainting at death. So woe to them! (20)
طَاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ ۚ فَإِذَا عَزَمَ ٱلْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ (٢١)
Obedience and upright speech.... So when the matter has been resolved upon [concerning going to war], if they remain true to Allah that will surely be better for them. (21)
فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوٓا۟ أَرْحَامَكُمْ (٢٢)
May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and ill-treat your blood relations? (22)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٣)
They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their sight. (23)
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَآ (٢٤)
Do they not contemplate the Quran, or are there locks on the hearts? (24)

Commentary

They Are Even Terrified of the Name of Jihad!

These verses clarify the differing stances of the "believers" and the "hypocrites" regarding the command for Jihad, serving as a completion to the discussions about these two groups in the preceding verses.

First, It says: "The believers constantly say, 'Why has a Surah not been sent down?'" (وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ).

A Surah that contains the command for Jihad and clarifies our duty against heartless, bloodthirsty, and irrational enemies; a Surah whose verses sprinkle the light of guidance upon our hearts and illuminate our souls with its radiance.

This is the state of the true believers.

But as for the hypocrites: "When a precise and firm Surah is sent down in which fighting (Jihad) is mentioned, you see those in whose hearts is a disease looking at you with a look of one fainting at the brink of death, with stunned gazes and eyes that have stopped moving in their sockets!" (فَإِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُحْكَمَةٌ وَ ذُكِرَ فِيهَا الْقِتالُ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ الْمَغْشِيِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَوْتِ‌).

Upon hearing the name of war, such terror and anxiety overwhelm them that they are nearly ready to give up the ghost! Their thoughts cease to function, the pupils of their eyes stop moving, and like those whose souls are about to be seized, they have a motionless and staring gaze without even blinking. This is the most eloquent expression of the state of cowardly and faint-hearted hypocrites!

Why should the reaction of the believers and the hypocrites not be so different, while the first group, due to their firm faith, both hope for the grace, favor, and help of the Lord, and do not fear martyrdom in His path?

For them, the field of Jihad is a field to express love for the Beloved, a field of honor and virtue, a field for the blossoming of talents, and a field of steadfastness, resistance, and victory; in such a field, fear has no meaning.

But for the "hypocrites," it is a field of death, destruction, and misery; a field of defeat and separation from worldly pleasures; a dark and gloomy field with a terrifying and ambiguous future!

According to some commentators, the meaning of "Surah Muhkamah" (a precise Surah) refers to Surahs in which the issue of Jihad is raised. However, there is no evidence for this specific interpretation; rather, it appears that "Muhkam" here carries its literal meaning of firm, solid, decisive, and free from any ambiguity—which is sometimes contrasted with the term Mutashabih (ambiguous). Of course, since verses of Jihad usually possess extraordinary decisiveness, they have a greater affinity with the concept of this word, but it is not limited to them.

The expression "those in whose hearts is a disease" (الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ) is a term usually used in the language of the Quran for the "hypocrites." The possibility suggested by some commentators that it refers to those "weak in faith" is consistent neither with other verses of the Quran nor with the verses before and after the verse under discussion, all of which speak of the hypocrites.

In any case, at the end of the verse, it says in a short sentence: "Woe to them, for death and destruction are better for them than life" (فَأَوْلى‌ لَهُمْ‌).

The phrase "Awla lahum" in Arabic literature is usually used as a threat, a curse, and a wish for distress and misfortune upon someone. [1] Some have also interpreted it to mean "Death is better for them" (al-mawtu awla lahum), and there is no obstacle to combining these meanings as we did in the commentary of the verse.

In the next verse, It adds: "If they were to obey, and refrain from defying the command of Jihad, and speak good, measured, and appropriate words, it would be better for them" (طاعَةٌ وَ قَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ‌). [2] The expression "Ma'ruf" (good/appropriate) speech might be in contrast to the discordant and evil words that the hypocrites uttered after the revelation of the verses of Jihad. Sometimes they would say: "Do not go forth in the heat" (At-Tawbah, 81).

And sometimes they would say: "Allah and His Messenger promised us nothing but delusions of victory!" (Al-Ahzab, 12).

And sometimes, to weaken the believers and hold them back from the battlefield, they would say: "Come to us [and be happy]!" (Al-Ahzab, 18).

Not only did they not encourage people toward Jihad, but they also strove hard to undermine their morale.

Then It adds: "But when the matter is determined and the command for Jihad becomes decisive, if they were true to Allah and acted with sincerity and purity, it would be better for them" (فَإِذا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللَّهَ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ‌).

This would lead to their honor in this world, and in the Hereafter, they would attain a great reward and supreme success.

The phrase "Azama al-amr" originally refers to a matter becoming firm and decided, but by the context of the preceding and succeeding verses, it refers to "Jihad."

In the next verse, It adds: "But if you turn away and choose the path of opposition, turning your backs on the command of Allah and acting upon His Book, is it to be expected of you other than that you will spread corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship?" (فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَنْ تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ تُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحامَكُمْ‌). [1]

For if you turn away from the Quran and Monotheism, you will certainly return to the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), and the programs of Jahiliyyah were nothing but "corruption in the land," "murder, plunder, and bloodshed," and "killing relatives and daughters."

This is if "Tawallaytum" is derived from the root Tawalli, meaning to turn away. However, many commentators have suggested the possibility that it is from the root Wilayah, meaning "governance" or "authority"—meaning: if the reins of government fall into your hands, nothing is expected of you but ruin, bloodshed, and the severing of kinship ties.

It seems a group of hypocrites had fabricated this excuse to flee the battlefield: "How can we step into the field of battle and shed blood and kill our relatives and be 'corrupters in the land'?"

The Quran responds to them: "On the day when authority was in your hands, did you do anything other than corruption, bloodshed, and severing kinship? These are mere excuses. The goal of war in Islam is to extinguish the fire of sedition, not corruption and ruin in the land; the goal is to dismantle the apparatus of oppression, not to sever ties of kinship."

In some narrations found in the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read: "This verse is about the Umayyads, who, when they took the reins of government, showed mercy neither to the young nor the old, even dragging their own relatives through blood and dust!" [1] It is clear that the Umayyads, from Abu Sufyan to his children and grandchildren, were all clear examples of this verse; the narration intends this, while the verse has a broad meaning that includes all oppressive and corrupt hypocrites.

In the next verse, It describes the final fate of this group of hypocrites and corrupt excuse-seekers as follows: "They are those whom Allah has cursed and removed from His mercy, making them deaf and blinding their eyes," so they neither hear any truth nor see any reality (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَ أَعْمى‌ أَبْصارَهُمْ‌).

They consider Islamic Jihad, which is based on the criteria of truth and justice, to be the severing of kinship and corruption in the land, yet they view all those crimes they committed in the Jahiliyyah, the blood of the innocent they shed during their rule, and the innocent infants they buried under the earth with their own hands as being in accordance with truth and justice! May the curse of Allah be upon them, for they have neither hearing ears nor seeing eyes!

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read that he said to his son Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "Beware of befriending one who severs ties of kinship, for I have found him cursed in the Book of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, in three places: Allah said: 'فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ...'" [1] "Rahm" (kinship) originally means the "womb" in the mother's belly, then this term was applied to all relatives because they originate from a single womb.

In another hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Three groups will not enter Paradise: the habitual drunkard, the practitioner of sorcery, and the one who severs ties of kinship." [2]

It goes without saying that Allah's cursing and rejection of this group, as well as taking away their power to perceive truths, never implies compulsion (jabr), because these are the punishments for their own actions and the reaction to their own conduct and behavior.

In the last verse under discussion, It mentions the real cause of the deviation of this wretched people, saying: "Do they not then reflect upon the verses of the Quran (to realize the truth and fulfill their duties), or are there locks upon their hearts?" (أَ فَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ أَمْ عَلى‌ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفالُها).

Yes, the factor of their misery is one of two things: either they do not reflect upon the Quran—this program of divine guidance and this complete healing prescription—or if they do reflect, due to their whims and the deeds they have previously committed, there are locks upon their hearts such that no truth can penetrate them.

In other words, if someone loses their way in the darkness, they either do not have a lamp in their hand or their eyes are blind; for if there is both a lamp and a seeing eye, finding the way is easy everywhere.

"Aqfal" is the plural of "Qufl" (lock), originally from the root Qufool meaning to return, or Qafeel meaning dry objects. Since when a door is closed and locked, anyone who comes there returns, and like a dry and solid object nothing penetrates it, this word is used for this specific tool.

Key Points

1- The Quran: A Book of Thought and Action

Various verses of the Quran reveal the truth that this great Heavenly Book is not merely for recitation; rather, its ultimate goal is "Dhikr" (remembrance), "Tadabbur" (reflecting on consequences and results), "Inzar" (warning), "bringing humans out of darkness into light," and "healing, mercy, and guidance."

In Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 50, we read: "And this is a blessed Reminder which We have sent down."

In Surah Sad, verse 29, we read: "A Book We have sent down to you, full of blessings, that they may reflect upon its verses."

In Surah Al-An'am, verse 19, it says: "And this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you with it and whomever it reaches."

In Surah Ibrahim, verse 1, He says: "A Book which We have sent down to you that you might bring mankind out of darknesses into the light."

And finally, in Surah Al-Isra, verse 82, it says: "And We send down of the Quran that which is a healing and a mercy for the believers."

Accordingly, the Holy Quran must be placed at the center of the lives of Muslims; they should take it as their model and example, implement its commands precisely, and align all aspects of their lives with it.

But unfortunately, the approach of a group of Muslims toward the Quran is like dealing with a set of incomprehensible incantations; they only engage in superficial recitation, and at most, they give importance to Tajweed, the points of articulation of letters, and the beauty of the voice. The greatest misery of Muslims stems from this very point: that they have removed the Quran from its form as a comprehensive life program and have contented themselves only with its words.

It is noteworthy that in the verses under discussion, it explicitly states that these diseased-hearted hypocrites did not reflect upon the Quran, which is why they fell into this dark state.

"Tadabbur" is from the root Dubur, meaning to examine the results and consequences of something, as opposed to Tafakkur (thinking), which is mostly used for examining the causes and reasons of something. The use of both terms in the Holy Quran is very meaningful.

However, it must not be forgotten that benefiting from the Quran requires a type of self-purification—though the Quran itself helps in self-purification—because if there are locks on the hearts, locks of whims and desires, pride and arrogance, stubbornness and prejudice, they will not allow the light of truth to enter. This point is also alluded to in the verses under discussion.

How beautifully Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام says in the sermon describing the qualities of the pious:

"As for the night, they stand on their feet, reciting the parts of the Quran in a measured tone, saddening their souls with it, and seeking from it the cure for their ailments. When they come across a verse containing encouragement, they lean toward it with longing, and their souls gaze at it with yearning, as if it were before their very eyes. And when they come across a verse containing fear and warning, they turn the ears of their hearts toward it, thinking that the roaring of Hell and its flames are echoing in the depths of their ears." [1]

2- A Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام)

In the interpretation of the phrase "or are there locks upon their hearts," it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Indeed, you have a heart and ears, and when Allah intends to guide a servant (due to his piety), He opens the ears of his heart, and when He intends otherwise for him, He seals the ears of his heart, so he will never be reformed; and that is the word of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic: 'or are there locks upon their hearts'." [1]

[^1]: Some have expressed that the meaning of the phrase becomes "followed by something disliked" and have considered it equivalent to "Woe to them." [^2]: "Ta'atun" is the subject (mubtada) and its predicate (khabar) is omitted, estimated as: "Obedience and appropriate speech would be better for them." Some have also considered it the predicate for an omitted subject, estimated as "Our command is obedience," but the first meaning is more appropriate. [^1]: Although few commentators have discussed the syntax of this verse, it appears that "in tawallaytum" is a conditional clause placed between the noun of 'asa and its predicate, and the response to the condition is the entire phrase "is it to be expected of you that you will spread corruption in the land." The estimation would be: "If you turn away from the Book of Allah, is anything expected from you except corruption in the land?" [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 40. [^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on those whose company is disliked, Hadith 7. As for the other two verses mentioned in the hadith, one is verse 25 of Surah Ar-Ra'd and the other is 27 of Al-Baqarah; in one, the curse is mentioned explicitly, and in the other, implicitly. [^2]: Al-Khisal by Saduq, as cited in Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 5, p. 41. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 193, known as the Sermon of Hammam. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 41.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَى ۙ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٥)
Indeed those who turned their backs after the guidance had become clear to them, it was Satan who had seduced them, and he had given them [far-flung] hopes. (25)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا۟ مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِى بَعْضِ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرَارَهُمْ (٢٦)
That is because they said to those who loathed what Allah had sent down: ‘We shall obey you in some matters,’ and Allah knows their secret dealings. (26)
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٧)
But how will it be [with them] when the angels take them away, striking their faces and their backs?! (27)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَآ أَسْخَطَ ٱللَّهَ وَكَرِهُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٢٨)
That, because they pursued what displeased Allah, and loathed His pleasure. So He has made their works fail. (28)

Commentary

Why Do They Not Ponder the Quran?!

These verses continue the discussion regarding the hypocrites and their various stances. It says: "Indeed, those who turned back [to disbelief] after guidance had become clear to them—Satan enticed them and filled them with false hopes" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ ارْتَدُّوا عَلى‌ أَدْبارِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَى الشَّيْطانُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَ أَمْلى‌ لَهُمْ‌).

Although some have suggested that this verse speaks about a group of the People of the Book who, before the rise of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, used to enumerate his signs based on their divine scriptures and were eagerly awaiting him, but after his rise and the clarification of these signs, they turned their backs on him because desires and material interests prevented their faith.

However, the context provided by the preceding and succeeding verses clearly shows that this verse also speaks about the hypocrites who came close and clearly saw and heard the proofs of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله truthfulness, yet turned away due to whim-worship and satanic enticements.

"Sawwala" (سول) is derived from the root "su'l" (سؤل), meaning a need for which the human soul is greedy ^1. "Taswil" (تسويل) means to entice and encourage toward matters for which one is greedy. Attributing this to Satan is because of the whisperings he instills in the human soul, which prevent his guidance.

The phrase "wa amla lahum" (و املى لهم) is from the root "imla'" (املاء), meaning the creation of long-term hopes and far-reaching desires that keep a person preoccupied and divert him from the Truth.

The next verse explains the reason for these satanic enticements and embellishments as follows: "That is because they said to those who detested what Allah revealed [to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of Islam], 'We will obey you in part of the matter'" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا ما نَزَّلَ اللَّهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ). This is the work of the hypocrite: he seeks out disillusioned and opposing individuals, and even if he does not share common ground in all aspects, he cooperates—or rather obeys—to the extent that commonalities exist.

The hypocrites of Medina also went to the Jews—the Jews of "Bani Nadir" and "Bani Qurayza" who were promoters of Islam before the Prophet's mission. However, after his appearance, they found the emergence of Islam unpleasant due to envy, pride, and the endangering of their interests. Since opposition to the Prophet of Islam and plotting against him was a commonality between the hypocrites and the Jews, they promised them cooperation.

The expression "in part of the matter" (فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ) perhaps indicates that "we only cooperate with you in this part; however, you oppose idolatry and believe in the Resurrection, and we do not agree with you in these matters" ^1. This statement is similar to what appears in verse 11 of Surah Al-Hashr:

^1: In the commentary of this verse, several other possibilities have been suggested, none of which are consistent with the preceding and succeeding verses; therefore, they were omitted.

"Have you not considered those who practice hypocrisy, saying to their brothers who have disbelieved among the People of the Book, 'If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will not obey anyone against you, ever; and if you are fought, we will surely aid you'!" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَ لا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَ إِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ‌).

At the end of the verse, He threatens them with a short phrase, saying: "And Allah knows their secrets" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرارَهُمْ‌).

He is aware of both their internal disbelief and hypocrisy, as well as their plotting with the help of the Jews, and He will punish them in due time.

He is also aware of what the Jews hid of envy, enmity, and stubbornness. According to the testimony of their own Book, they were so aware of the signs of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that they recognized him as they recognized their own sons, and they used to disclose these signs previously; but after his appearance, they hid and concealed them all. Allah is aware of this concealment.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام, it is stated that the meaning of "those who detested what Allah revealed" (كَرِهُوا ما نَزَّلَ اللَّهُ‌) refers to the "Umayyads" who disliked the revelation of Allah's command regarding the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام ^1. It is clear that this is a form of application and identification of an instance, not a limitation of the verse's concept.

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation for this implicit threat, saying: "Then how [will it be] when the angels take them in death, striking their faces and their backs?" (فَكَيْفَ إِذا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَ أَدْبارَهُمْ‌) ^1. Yes, these angels are commissioned to begin their punishment at the threshold of death so they may taste the bitter flavor of disbelief, hypocrisy, obstinacy, and malice. They strike their faces because they turned toward the enemies of Allah, and they strike their backs because they turned their backs on the divine verses and His Prophet.

This meaning is similar to what appears in verse 50 of Surah Al-Anfal regarding the disbelievers and hypocrites: "And if you could but see when the angels take the souls of those who disbelieved... striking their faces and their backs and [saying], 'Taste the punishment of the Burning Fire'" (وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَ أَدْبارَهُمْ وَ ذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ‌).

In the final verse under discussion, He again explains the reason for this divine punishment at the threshold of their death, saying: "That is because they followed what angered Allah and detested His pleasure, so He rendered their deeds void" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اتَّبَعُوا ما أَسْخَطَ اللَّهَ وَ كَرِهُوا رِضْوانَهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمالَهُمْ‌). This is because the condition for the acceptance of deeds and any effort or striving is the pleasure of Allah. Therefore, it is natural that the deeds of those who persist in the anger of Allah and oppose His pleasure should be destroyed, and they bid farewell to this world empty-handed and with a massive burden of sins.

The state of this group is exactly the opposite of the state of the believers, whom the angels of mercy come to welcome at the threshold of death, saying to them with an open face:

"Peace be upon you; enter Paradise for what you used to do" (سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌). "Those whom the angels take in death while they are good..." (الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ‌) (An-Nahl: 32).

It is noteworthy that regarding divine anger, the phrase is used in the verbal form (ما أَسْخَطَ اللَّهَ‌ - what angered Allah), while regarding His pleasure, it is used in the nominal form (رِضْوانَهُ‌ - His pleasure).

Some commentators have said this difference in expression contains a subtle point: that Allah's anger is occasional, while His pleasure and mercy are continuous and perpetual.

It is also clear that anger, wrath, and displeasure in the case of Allah do not mean a psychological affection, just as His pleasure does not mean an expansion of the soul. Rather, as stated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"The anger of Allah is His punishment, and His pleasure is His reward" (غضب اللَّه عقابه و رضاه ثوابه‌) ^1.

^1: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 105.

^1: Tawhid al-Saduq, as quoted in Al-Mizan, Vol. 18, p. 266.

Ayahs

أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَن لَّن يُخْرِجَ ٱللَّهُ أَضْغَـٰنَهُمْ (٢٩)
Do those in whose hearts is a sickness suppose that Allah will not expose their spite? (29)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَأَرَيْنَـٰكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ ۚ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِى لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ (٣٠)
If We wish, We will show them to you so that you recognize them by their mark. Yet you will recognize them by their tone of speech, and Allah knows your deeds. (30)
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا۟ أَخْبَارَكُمْ (٣١)
We will surely test you until We ascertain those of you who wage jihad and those who are steadfast, and We shall appraise your record. (31)

Commentary

Hypocrites Can Be Recognized by the Tone of Their Speech

In these verses, the discussion turns once again to another set of characteristics and signs of the hypocrites. It specifically emphasizes that they should not imagine they can forever conceal their inner reality from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, or escape a great scandal.

First, it says: "Or do those in whose hearts is a disease think that Allah will never bring to light their [deep-seated] hatreds?" (أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ أَنْ لَنْ يُخْرِجَ اللَّهُ أَضْغانَهُمْ) [1]. Adghān is the plural of dighn, meaning intense hatred or malice.

Indeed, they harbored intense hatred in their hearts toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, always waiting for an opportunity to strike a blow against them. The Quran warns them not to think they can always keep their true faces hidden.

Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "And if We willed, We could show them to you, and you would know them by their marks" (وَ لَوْ نَشاءُ لَأَرَيْناكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ). We would place a mark upon their faces so that by observing it, you would become aware of their hypocrisy and see them with your own eyes.

Then it adds: "But you will surely know them by the tone of [their] speech" (وَ لَتَعْرِفَنَّهّمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ).

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Lahn consists of diverting speech from its standard rules and norms, or giving it incorrect inflection, or shifting it from a literal form to allusion and metaphor." The meaning intended in the verse under discussion is this third meaning.

[1]: Some have considered "am" in the above verse to be "interrogative," while others consider it "disjunctive" meaning "rather" (bal), but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

That is, these diseased-hearted hypocrites can be recognized by their insinuations, stings, and their malicious and hypocritical expressions.

Wherever there is talk of Jihad, they strive in some way to weaken the people's will; wherever there is talk of truth and justice, they divert it elsewhere; and where the virtuous, the pure, and the pioneers of Islam are mentioned, they try in some way to stain them and diminish their credibility.

Hence, it is narrated in a famous Hadith from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri who said: "The 'tone of speech' (lahn al-qawl) was their hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib, and we used to recognize the hypocrites during the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) by their hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib." [1] Indeed, this was one of the prominent signs of the hypocrites—that they bore enmity toward the first believer among men and the first pioneering self-sacrificer of Islam.

Fundamentally, it is impossible for a person to hold something in their heart and be able to conceal it for a long time such that it does not appear even in their allusions, hints, and the tone of their speech. Thus, we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام): "No one conceals a thing..."

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses in question. Incidentally, the content of this Hadith has been narrated by a group of prominent Sunni scholars in their books, including Ahmad in Fada'il, Ibn Abd al-Barr in al-Isti'ab, al-Dhahabi in Tarikh al-Islam, Ibn al-Athir in Jami' al-Usul, al-Ganji al-Shafi'i in Kifayat al-Talib, Muhibb al-Din al-Tabari in Riyad al-Nadirah, al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and others. This indicates it is one of the established narrations from the Noble Prophet of Islam (for further explanation, refer to Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, p. 110 onwards).

"...except that it manifests in the slips of his tongue and the expressions of his face." [1] In other verses of the Quran, the malicious words of the hypocrites—which are instances of this "tone of speech"—or their suspicious movements are recorded. Perhaps for this reason, some commentators have said that after the revelation of this verse, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recognized the hypocrites well by their signs.

A clear witness to this is that he was commanded that if one of them died, he should never offer the funeral prayer for him, nor stand by his grave to pray for forgiveness: "And do not pray [the funeral prayer] over any of them who has died - ever - and do not stand at his grave" (At-Tawbah: 84).

The times when the hypocrites most clearly revealed their true faces were during Jihad: before the war when collecting aid and preparing for the battlefield; on the battlefield during the enemy's intense attacks; and after the war during the distribution of spoils. These are mentioned in numerous verses of the Holy Quran, especially in Surah At-Tawbah and Al-Ahzab. It reached a point where even ordinary Muslims recognized the hypocrites in these scenes.

Today, too, identifying hypocrites by the tone of their speech and their contrary stances on important social issues—especially during crises or wars—is not difficult. With a little attention, they can be identified by their words and behavior. It is well that Muslims remain vigilant, take inspiration from this verse, and recognize and expose this dangerous and spiteful group.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "And Allah knows [all] your deeds" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمالَكُمْ).

He knows both the hidden and manifest deeds of the believers, as well as the deeds of the hypocrites. Even if they can hide their true faces from the people, can they conceal them from Allah, who is with them in the manifest and the hidden, in private and in public?!

In the next verse, for further emphasis and to show the ways of distinguishing believers from hypocrites, it adds: "And We will surely test you until We make evident those who strive among you [for the cause of Allah] and the patient" (وَ لَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّى نَعْلَمَ الْمُجاهِدِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَ الصَّابِرِينَ), and [distinguish them from] the pseudo-mujahideen and the weak-willed hypocrites.

Although this test has a broad scope and includes patience in fulfilling all duties, given the word mujahideen and the preceding and following verses, it primarily refers to testing on the field of Jihad. Truly, the field of Jihad is a field of great and difficult testing, and few can keep their true faces hidden from view in such arenas.

And at the end of the verse, He says: In addition to testing you, "We will test your reports" (وَ نَبْلُوَا أَخْبارَكُمْ).

Many commentators have said that "reports" (akhbār) here refers to human actions, because when an action is performed by a person, it spreads among the people as a "report." Some have also said "reports" refers to inner secrets, as people's actions report these secrets.

It is also possible that "reports" here means the news people give about their own state or their commitments. For example, the hypocrites had pledged to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that they would not turn their backs on the battlefield, yet they broke their pledge: "And they had already promised Allah before not to turn their backs and flee" (Al-Ahzab: 15).

Also, "a party of them asked permission of the Prophet [to leave], saying, 'Indeed, our houses are unprotected,' while they were not unprotected. They desired not except to flee" (Al-Ahzab: 13).

In this way, Allah tests both the actions of humans and their words and reports. According to this interpretation, these two phrases in the verse have two different meanings, whereas according to previous interpretations, they reinforce one another.

In any case, this is not the first time Allah announces to the people that "We test you so that your ranks may be distinguished," and the true believers may be known from those weak in faith and the hypocrites. This issue of trial and testing is raised in numerous verses of the Quran.

We have discussed matters related to Divine testing in Volume 1 under verse 155 of Al-Baqarah (pages 524 to 535) and also in Volume 16 at the beginning of Surah Al-Ankabut in detail.

Incidentally, the phrase "until We know those who strive among you" (حَتَّى نَعْلَمَ الْمُجاهِدِينَ مِنْكُمْ) does not mean that Allah is unaware of this group; rather, it refers to the realization of this Divine knowledge and the manifestation of such individuals—that is, until this Divine knowledge finds external realization and objectivity, and the ranks become distinguished.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَشَآقُّوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٣٢)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become clear to them, they will not hurt Allah in the least, and He shall make their works fail. (32)
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَلَا تُبْطِلُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ (٣٣)
O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle, and do not render your works void. (33)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ مَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ (٣٤)
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and then die faithless, Allah will never forgive them. (34)

Commentary

Those Who Die in a State of Disbelief Will Never Be Forgiven

Following the various discussions regarding the condition of the hypocrites in the preceding verses, these verses discuss another group of disbelievers, stating: "Indeed, those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah and oppose the Messenger after guidance has become clear to them—never will they harm Allah at all, and He will render their deeds worthless" (even if they have performed good deeds, since they were not coupled with faith, they are nullified) (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ شَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدى‌ لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللَّهَ شَيْئاً وَ سَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ‌).

This group might be the polytheists of Mecca, or the disbelievers among the Jews of Medina, or both, because the expressions "disbelief" (kufr), "averting from the way of Allah" (sadd 'an sabilillah), and "opposing the Messenger" (shaqqu al-Rasul) have appeared in Quranic verses regarding both groups.

The expression "guidance becoming clear" (tabayyun al-huda) in the case of the Meccan polytheists was through miracles, and in the case of the disbelievers among the People of the Book, it was through their own scripture.

The "nullification of their deeds" (habt) either refers to the good deeds they occasionally performed—such as hospitality, charity, and helping the wayfarer—or it refers to the failure of their plots against Islam.

In any case, this group possessed three characteristics: "disbelief," "averting from the way of Allah," and "enmity and hostility toward the Prophet." One was opposition to Allah, the second to the servants of Allah, and the third to the Messenger of Allah.

In the next verse, the address is turned toward the believers. After explaining the lines of the hypocrites and disbelievers, He defines their path as follows: "O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and do not invalidate your deeds" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَ لا تُبْطِلُوا أَعْمالَكُمْ‌).

In reality, the program of the believers is the polar opposite of the disbelieving and hypocritical group in every aspect. Those oppose Allah's command, while these obey; those rise in enmity and hostility toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, while these are submissive; those have their deeds destroyed due to disbelief, hypocrisy, and reminding others of favors, but these—by abandoning such things—have the reward of their deeds preserved in the presence of Allah.

In any case, the tone of the verse indicates that among the believers of that day, there were individuals who were negligent in the matter of obeying Allah and the Messenger and in protecting their deeds from being invalidated, whom Allah warns with this verse.

Evidence for this is the occasion of revelation (shan nuzul) that some commentators have narrated regarding this verse: that the "Bani Asad" embraced Islam and presented themselves to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saying, "We have preferred you over ourselves, and placed our lives and families at your disposal," but a tone of reminding him of their favor was perceived from their speech. The above verse was revealed and warned them in this regard.

Some jurists (fuqaha) have used the final phrase ("and do not invalidate your deeds") to argue for the prohibition of breaking one's prayer (salat), but as the preceding and following verses—and the verse under discussion itself—testify, it is not related to this meaning. Rather, the goal is the non-invalidation of deeds through polytheism, hypocrisy, reminding of favors, and the like.

The last verse under discussion is an explanation and emphasis for what appeared in the previous verses regarding the disbelievers, and meanwhile, it shows the way of return to those who desire it, stating: "Indeed, those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah and then die while they are disbelievers—never will Allah forgive them" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ صَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ ماتُوا وَ هُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ‌).

This is because with death, the doors of repentance are closed. These people carry the heavy burden of their own disbelief and the misguidance of others on their shoulders; how is it possible for Allah to forgive them?

Thus, in the sum of these verses, three groups have been mentioned: the hypocrites, the disbelievers, and the believers, and the characteristics and fate of each have been specified.

Key Points Factors in the Destruction of the Reward of Deeds

One of the sensitive points warned about in various verses of the Quran, including the verse under discussion, is that believers must be careful that their deeds do not become nullified (habt) like those of the disbelievers.

In other words, the essence of the deed is one thing, and its preservation is a more important matter. A pure, sound, and beneficial deed is one that is sound and flawless from the beginning and is protected and guarded until the end of one's life.

The factors that endanger or destroy human deeds are many, including:

1- "Reminding of favors and causing offense," as the Quran says: "O you who have believed, do not invalidate your charities with reminders [of favor] or injury as does one who spends his wealth [only] to be seen by the people and does not believe in Allah and the Last Day" (Al-Baqarah: 264). Here, two factors for the invalidation of deeds are mentioned: one is "reminders and injury" and the other is "hypocrisy and disbelief." The first comes after the deed, and the second accompanies it, and they set good deeds on fire.

2- "Self-conceit" (ujb) is another factor for the destruction of the effects of a deed, as stated in the hadith: "Self-conceit consumes good deeds just as fire consumes wood!" [1]

3- "Envy" (hasad) is also one of these types of actions, regarding which an expression similar to "self-conceit" has been used, stating that it destroys good deeds like fire. The Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Beware of envy, for envy consumes good deeds just as fire consumes wood." [2] Fundamentally, just as good deeds erase evil deeds (Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds - Surah Hud: 114), sometimes evil deeds also completely nullify the effect of good deeds.

4- The issue of preserving faith until the end of life is the most important condition for the permanence of the effects of deeds, as the Quran explicitly says: "Those who die without faith, all their deeds are nullified." [3]

[1]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 8, p. 522. [2]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 73, p. 255. [3]: Az-Zumar: 65.

It is from here that we realize the importance and difficulties of the issue of preserving deeds. Therefore, it is narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "Preserving a deed is harder than the deed itself." The narrator asked: "What is meant by preserving the deed?" He replied: "A man gives a gift or spends an amount for Allah alone without any partner, and it is recorded for him as a secret deed. Then he mentions it, so it is erased and recorded for him as a public deed. Then he mentions it again, so it is erased and recorded for him as hypocrisy (riya)!" [1]

The verse under discussion makes a concise reference to all these matters, saying: "And do not invalidate your deeds." [2]

[1]: "Al-Kafi," Vol. 2, Chapter on Hypocrisy, Hadith 16. [2]: For a more detailed explanation regarding the issue of nullification (habt), please study Volume 2, page 70, under verse 217 of Al-Baqarah.

Ayah

فَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ وَتَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّلْمِ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَكُمْ وَلَن يَتِرَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ (٣٥)
So do not slacken and [do not] call for peace when you have the upper hand and Allah is with you, and He will not stint [the reward of] your works. (35)

Commentary

Unjust and Humiliating Peace!

Following the previous verses regarding the issue of Jihad, this verse points to one of the important points concerning "Jihad," which is that weak-willed individuals and those with frail faith often propose the issue of peace to escape the burden of Jihad and the hardships of the battlefield. Certainly, peace is very good, but in its proper place—a peace that secures lofty Islamic goals and preserves the dignity, greatness, and honor of Muslims, not a peace that drags them into humiliation and abasement.

Therefore, He says: "Now that you have heard the previous commands, do not weaken and do not invite the enemies to peace while you are superior" (فَلا تَهِنُوا وَ تَدْعُوا إِلَى السَّلْمِ وَ أَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ) ^1. This means: now that the signs of your victory and superiority have become manifest, how can you render your victories fruitless by proposing peace, the meaning of which is retreat and defeat?

In reality, this is not peace; it is a surrender and compromise that stems from weakness and vileness. It is a form of ugly comfort-seeking that brings about painful and dangerous consequences.

At the end of the verse, to strengthen the morale of the Mujahideen Muslims, He adds: "And Allah is with you, and He will never decrease the reward of your deeds" (وَ اللَّهُ مَعَكُمْ وَ لَنْ يَتِرَكُمْ أَعْمالَكُمْ). One who has Allah with Him has all the factors of victory at His disposal; He never feels alone, does not allow weakness or lethargy to creep in, does not surrender to the enemy in the name of peace, and does not squander the fruits of the martyrs' blood at sensitive moments.

"لن يتركم" is derived from the root "watr" (rhyming with satr), meaning "solitary" or "individual." Thus, those who lose some of their close relatives and are left alone are called "witr" (rhyming with fikr). It also carries the meaning of "reduction" or "deficiency." In the verse under discussion, it is a beautiful metaphor for the fact that Allah will not leave you alone and will keep the reward and recompense of your deeds with you.

Especially since you know that every step you take in the path of Jihad is recorded for you; not only will He not diminish anything from your reward, but He will also increase it from His grace and bounty.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the verse under discussion has no contradiction with verse 61 of Surah Al-Anfal, where He says: وَ إِنْ جَنَحُوا لِلسَّلْمِ فَاجْنَحْ لَها وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ: "And if they incline to peace, then incline you also to it, and trust in Allah; surely He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing," such that we would consider one as abrogating the other.

Rather, each of these two refers to a specific case: one points to "rational peace" and the other to "improper peace." One is a peace that fully secures the interests of Muslims, and the other is a peace proposed by weak and lethargic Muslims on the verge of victory.

Therefore, following the verse in Surah Al-Anfal, He says: وَ إِنْ يُرِيدُوا أَنْ يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ اللَّهُ: "But if they intend to deceive you [by proposing peace] and there is a trick involved, never surrender and do not worry, for Allah is your supporter."

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in his command to Malik al-Ashtar, referred to both types of peace, saying:

و لا تدفعن صلحا دعاك اليه عدوك و للَّه فيه رضا

"And do not reject a peace to which your enemy invites you and in which there is the pleasure of Allah." [^2] The proposal of peace coming from the enemy's side on one hand, and it being coupled with Allah's pleasure on the other, indicates the division of peace into the two types mentioned above.

In any case, the leaders of Muslims must be exceptionally precise and vigilant in identifying the instances of peace and war, which are among the most complex and delicate fate-determining issues, because the slightest error in calculation in this regard brings about deadly consequences.

[^2]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Letter 53.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۚ وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمْ أُجُورَكُمْ وَلَا يَسْـَٔلْكُمْ أَمْوَٰلَكُمْ (٣٦)
The life of the world is just play and diversion, but if you are faithful and Godwary, He will give you your rewards, and will not ask your wealth [in return] from you. (36)
إِن يَسْـَٔلْكُمُوهَا فَيُحْفِكُمْ تَبْخَلُوا۟ وَيُخْرِجْ أَضْغَـٰنَكُمْ (٣٧)
Should He ask it from you and press you, you will be stingy, and He will expose your spite. (37)
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَمِنكُم مَّن يَبْخَلُ ۖ وَمَن يَبْخَلْ فَإِنَّمَا يَبْخَلُ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْغَنِىُّ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ ۚ وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم (٣٨)
Ah! There you are, being invited to spend in the way of Allah; yet among you there are those who are stingy; and whoever is stingy is stingy only to himself. Allah is the All-sufficient, and you are all-needy, and if you turn away He will replace you with another people, and they will not be like you. (38)

Commentary

If You Turn Away, He Will Grant This Mission to Another Group

We have stated that Surah "Muhammad" صلى الله عليه وآله is the Surah of "Jihad"; it begins with the subject of Jihad and concludes with the subject of Jihad.

The verses under discussion, which are the final verses of this Surah, address another aspect of human life in this regard. To further encourage and motivate Muslims toward obedience to Allah in general, and the issue of Jihad in particular, it highlights the worthlessness of the worldly life. This is because a major deterrent from Jihad is being preoccupied with and attached to the material life of this world.
