He says: "The life of this world is only play and amusement" (إِنَّمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا لَعِبٌ وَ لَهْوٌ).

"La'ib" (play) refers to actions that possess a kind of imaginary order to reach an imaginary goal, and "Lahw" (amusement) refers to any activity that keeps a person preoccupied and diverts them from fundamental matters.

Truly, the life of this world is "play" and "amusement"; it yields neither quality nor lasting state; it has neither permanence nor stability. It consists of fleeting moments and unstable pleasures accompanied by various troubles! Following this, He adds: "And if you believe and be God-fearing, He will give you your rewards in full and fittingly, and He does not ask you for your [entire] wealth" (وَ إِنْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَ تَتَّقُوا يُؤْتِكُمْ أُجُورَكُمْ وَ لا يَسْئَلْكُمْ أَمْوالَكُمْ) [1].

[1]: The phrase "لا يسئلكم" is in the jussive mood (majzum), as it is conjoined to the result of the conditional sentence, which is "يؤتكم".

Neither does Allah ask you for wealth in exchange for guidance and those great rewards in this world and the Hereafter, nor does His Prophet. Fundamentally, Allah has no need, and the need of His Prophet is only for Allah.

And if a small portion of your wealth is taken as Zakat or other religious dues, that too is spent for your own benefit—for the maintenance of your orphans, the needy, and the wayfarers, and for defending the security and independence of your country, establishing order and peace, providing for necessities, and the development and prosperity of your cities and lands.

Therefore, even this amount is for yourselves, as Allah and His Prophet are independent of everyone. Thus, there is no contradiction between the concept of this verse and the verses regarding Infaq (spending), Zakat, and the like.

In the commentary of the phrase "وَ لا يَسْئَلْكُمْ أَمْوالَكُمْ" and the resolution of the potential contradiction, several other possibilities have been suggested:

Some have said: He does not demand any of your wealth in exchange for guidance and reward.

Others have said: He does not demand all of your wealth, but only a portion of it.

Some have also said: This phrase indicates that all wealth belongs to Allah, even though "for a few days, this trust is with us."

However, the first commentary is the most appropriate of all.

In any case, it must not be forgotten that a part of Jihad is "Jihad with wealth," and fundamentally, any battle with the enemy requires expenses that must be gathered from the faithful and God-fearing Muslims—those who are not dependent on or attached to the world. The verses under discussion, in fact, prepare the intellectual and cultural grounds for this issue.

In the next verse, to demonstrate the extent of most people's attachment to personal wealth and riches, He adds: "If He were to ask them of you and press you, you would withhold, and He would bring out your grudges [and anger]!" (إِنْ يَسْئَلْكُمُوها فَيُحْفِكُمْ تَبْخَلُوا وَ يُخْرِجْ أَضْغانَكُمْ).

"Yuhfikum" is derived from the root "Ihfa," meaning persistence in demanding and asking, and it originally comes from "Hafa," meaning to walk barefoot. This expression is a metaphor for actions that a person pursues to the ultimate limit; hence, "Ihfa al-sharib" means trimming the mustache to the shortest possible limit.

And "Adghan" is the plural of "Dighn," which, as previously mentioned, means "intense grudge."

In summary, the verse expresses the intense attachment of many people to financial matters and is, in fact, a kind of reproach and censure of them, while simultaneously encouraging the abandonment of this dependency—to the point that if even Allah were to demand it from them, they would harbor anger and a grudge against Him! In this way, with this whip of reproach, He awakens the sleeping souls of humans so they may remove the chains of captivity and slavery to wealth from their necks and become such that they sacrifice everything in the way of the Beloved, offering it all at His feet, seeking instead faith, Taqwa, and His pleasure and satisfaction.

The last verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, is another emphasis on what appeared in the previous verses regarding material matters, people's attachments to them, and spending in the way of Allah.

He says: "Behold, you are those who are called to spend in the way of Allah, but among you are those who are stingy (ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يَبْخَلُ).

Here, the question arises: if the previous verses stated that Allah does not demand your wealth, how is it that in this verse, the command is given for Infaq in the way of Allah?

The continuation of the verse itself provides the answer to this question in two ways: First, it says: "And whoever is stingy is only stingy toward himself" (وَ مَنْ يَبْخَلُ فَإِنَّما يَبْخَلُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ) [1]. This is because the result of spending returns to you in this world—class divides are reduced, peace and security prevail in society, and love, purity, and sincerity replace grudge and enmity; this is your worldly reward.

And in the Hereafter, in exchange for every Dirham and Dinar, He bestows upon you bounties and blessings that have never crossed a human mind. Therefore, however much you are stingy, you are being stingy toward yourselves! In other words, the issue of Infaq here refers mostly to spending for Jihad, and the expression "in the way of Allah" is consistent with this meaning. It is clear that any assistance toward the progress of Jihad guarantees the preservation of the existence, independence, and honor of a society.

[1]: The word "Bukhl" (stinginess) is sometimes transitive with "an" and sometimes with "ala." In the first case, it carries the meaning of withholding, and in the second, it means causing harm.

The second answer is that "Allah is the Self-Sufficient, and you are the needy" (وَ اللَّهُ الْغَنِيُّ وَ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ).

He is independent of both your spending and your obedience; it is you who, in this world and the Hereafter, are in need of His grace, mercy, and reward.

Fundamentally, all contingent beings and everything other than Allah are absolute poverty and need, and the Self-Sufficient by Essence is only Allah. They are constantly dependent on Him even for their very existence, receiving aid moment by moment from the eternal source of His grace; if He were to cut off His grace for a single moment, the existence of all would vanish and "the forms would collapse"! The final sentence is a warning to all Muslims to appreciate this great blessing and immense gift—that Allah has made you the guardians of His pure religion so that you may be the supporters of the faith and the helpers of His Prophet. If you do not value this great blessing, "and if you turn away, He will replace you with another people, and they will not be like you!" (وَ إِنْ تَتَوَلَّوْا يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَكُونُوا أَمْثالَكُمْ).

Yes, this burden will never remain on the ground. If you do not realize the importance of your position and ignore this great mission, Allah will raise another people and place this great mission upon their shoulders—a people who will be far superior to you in sacrifice, devotion, the offering of life and wealth, and spending in the way of Allah! This is a great threat, the like of which also appears in verse 54 of Surah Al-Ma'idah:

"O you who believe! Whoever among you turns back from his religion (he does not harm Allah), Allah will bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, humble toward the believers, stern toward the disbelievers, striving in the way of Allah and never fearing the blame of any blamer" (Al-Ma'idah: 54).

It is noteworthy that most commentators have narrated under the verse in question that after this verse was revealed, a group of the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "Who are these people whom Allah has mentioned in His Book?" At that moment, Salman was sitting near the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet tapped Salman's leg (and according to one narration, Salman's shoulder) and said:

"This man and his people. By the One in Whose hand is my soul, if faith were suspended from the Pleiades (Al-Thurayya), men from Persia would surely attain it!"

This Hadith and similar ones have been recorded by famous Sunni traditionists such as the renowned Al-Bayhaqi and Al-Tirmidhi in their famous books. Famous Shiite and Sunni commentators are in agreement upon it, such as the author of Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Ruh al-Bayan, Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr al-Razi, Al-Maraghi, Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, and others.

In the commentary "Durr al-Manthur," several Hadiths on this subject are also cited under this same verse [1]. Another Hadith is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام which complements the above Hadith; he said:

"By Allah, He fulfilled this promise of His and replaced them with a group from the non-Arabs (Mawali) who were better than them" [2].

[1]: "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 67.

[2]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 108.

If we look at the history of Islam and Islamic sciences carefully and free from any prejudice, and observe the contribution of the non-Arabs, especially the Persians, in the fields of Jihad and struggle against enemies on one hand, and the refinement and compilation of Islamic sciences on the other, we will realize the reality of this Hadith, and the explanation of this matter is extensive.

O Allah! Keep us firm and steadfast in the path of Jihad, sacrifice, and devotion in the way of Your pure religion. O Lord! Never strip from us this great honor You have bestowed upon us—to be the callers to Your pure religion.

O Lord! At this time when fierce storms have risen from the East and the West to erase the traces of Your pure religion, grant us greater strength, firmer faith, more sacrifice, and abundant sincerity! Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. Friday, 17th of Ramadan (the day of the Victory of Badr), year 1405, corresponding to 17/3/64 (June 7, 1985). End of Surah Muhammad (ص) and end of Volume 21 of Tafsir Nemouneh.
